title: Midwestern United States

infobox.value: * [East North Central](/wiki/East_North_Central_states "East North Central states") * [West North Central](/wiki/West_North_Central_states "West North Central states") * [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") * [Lower Midwest](#Cultural_overlap) * [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains")

* [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") as defined by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau").[[1]](#cite_note-CensusRegionsMap-1) Regional definitions might vary slightly among sources.

* [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area") * [Detroit](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit") * [Minneapolis–St. Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93St._Paul "Minneapolis–St. Paul") * [St. Louis](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis") * [Cincinnati](/wiki/Greater_Cincinnati "Greater Cincinnati") * [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City_metropolitan_area "Kansas City metropolitan area") * [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus_metropolitan_area,_Ohio "Columbus metropolitan area, Ohio") * [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area") * [Cleveland](/wiki/Greater_Cleveland "Greater Cleveland") * [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area")

* [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") * [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") * [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") * [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") * [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") * [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") * [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska") * [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") * [Wichita](/wiki/Wichita,_Kansas "Wichita, Kansas") * [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") * [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati") * [St. Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota") * [St. Louis](/wiki/Saint_Louis,_Missouri "Saint Louis, Missouri")

(also referred to as **the Midwest**, the **Heartland**, the **American Midwest**, **Middle America**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) or, datedly, the **Middle West**) is one of the four [census regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"). It occupies the northern central part of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[1]](#cite_note-CensusRegionsMap-1) It was officially named the **North Central Region** by the U.S. Census Bureau until 1984.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is between the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") and the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"), with [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to the north and the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") to the south.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Census region of the United States

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Midwest (disambiguation)](/wiki/Midwest_(disambiguation) "Midwest (disambiguation)"), [North Central Region (WFTDA)](/wiki/North_Central_Region_(WFTDA) "North Central Region (WFTDA)"), and [Mid-Western Regional Council](/wiki/Mid-Western_Regional_Council "Mid-Western Regional Council").

The **Midwestern United States** (also referred to as **the Midwest**, the **Heartland**, the **American Midwest**, **Middle America**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) or, datedly, the **Middle West**) is one of the four [census regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"). It occupies the northern central part of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[1]](#cite_note-CensusRegionsMap-1) It was officially named the **North Central Region** by the U.S. Census Bureau until 1984.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is between the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") and the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"), with [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to the north and the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") to the south.

The United States Census Bureau's definition consists of 12 states in the north central United States: [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") put the population of the Midwest at 68,995,685.[[4]](#cite_note-Census2020-4) The region generally lies on the broad [Interior Plain](/wiki/Interior_Plain "Interior Plain") between the states occupying the [Appalachian Mountain range](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") and the states occupying the [Rocky Mountain range](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"). Major rivers in the region include, from east to west, the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), the upper [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), and the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River").[[5]](#cite_note-Hobbs-5) The Midwest is further divided by the U.S. Census Bureau into the [East North Central states](/wiki/East_North_Central_states "East North Central states") and the [West North Central states](/wiki/West_North_Central_states "West North Central states").

[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") is the most populous city in the American Midwest and the third-most populous in the United States. Other large Midwestern cities include [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"), [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), [St. Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota"), and [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"). The [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area") has an estimated 9.4 million residents and is the region's largest [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"), as well as the [fourth-largest metropolitan area in North America](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_urban_agglomerations_in_North_America "List of the largest urban agglomerations in North America"). Other prominent metropolitan areas in the Midwest include [Metro Detroit](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit"), [Minneapolis–St. Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93St._Paul "Minneapolis–St. Paul"), [Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), the [Cincinnati metro area](/wiki/Cincinnati_metro_area "Cincinnati metro area"), the [Kansas City metro area](/wiki/Kansas_City_metropolitan_area "Kansas City metropolitan area"), the [Columbus metro area](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio_metropolitan_area "Columbus, Ohio metropolitan area"), the [Indianapolis metro area](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area"), [Greater Cleveland](/wiki/Greater_Cleveland "Greater Cleveland"), and the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area").

The region's economy is a mix of heavy industry and agriculture, with extensive areas forming part of the United States' [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt"). Finance and services such as medicine and education are becoming increasingly important. Its central location makes it a transportation crossroads for river boats, railroads, autos, trucks, and airplanes. Politically, the region includes multiple [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state"), and therefore is heavily contested and often decisive in elections.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## The West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The West")]

The term *West* was applied to the region in [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America") and in the early years of the United States, when the British colonial territories had not extended far from the Atlantic coast and the [Pacific seaboard](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") was generally unknown. By the early 19th century, anything west of the [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") was considered the [American frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"). Over time, the American frontier moved to west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). During the colonial period, the French settled the upper Mississippi watershed, which included the valleys of the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and the [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River"), and called it the [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country").[[8]](#cite_note-Ekberg-French_Roots-8) In 1787, the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance") was enacted, creating the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), which was bounded by the Great Lakes and the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Some entities in the Midwest have "Northwest" in their names for historical reasons, such as [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University") in Illinois.[[10]](#cite_note-Pridmore-10)

One of the earliest late 19th-century uses of *Midwest* was in reference to Kansas and Nebraska to indicate that they were the civilized areas of the West.[[11]](#cite_note-Blaser_1990_69-11) Another term applied to the same region is *[Heartland](/wiki/Heartland_(United_States) "Heartland (United States)")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

### Precolumbian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Precolumbian")]

Main article: [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture")

Among the [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), [Paleo-American](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") cultures were the earliest in North America, with a presence in the Great Plains and Great Lakes areas from about 12,000 BCE to around 8,000 BCE.[[13]](#cite_note-google-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Monks_Mound_-_Summer_2024.jpg/330px-Monks_Mound_-_Summer_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monks_Mound_-_Summer_2024.jpg)

[Monks Mound](/wiki/Monks_Mound "Monks Mound"), located at the [Cahokia Mounds](/wiki/Cahokia_Mounds "Cahokia Mounds") near [Collinsville, Illinois](/wiki/Collinsville,_Illinois "Collinsville, Illinois"), is the largest Precolumbian earthwork north of [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") and a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").

Following the Paleo-American period is the [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_the_Americas "Archaic period in the Americas") period (8,000 BCE to 1,000 BCE), the [Woodland Tradition](/wiki/Woodland_period "Woodland period") (1,000 BCE to 100 CE), and the Mississippian Period (900 to 1500 CE). Archeological evidence indicates that [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture") traits probably began in the [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri") area and spread northwest along the Mississippi and Illinois rivers and entered the state along the [Kankakee River](/wiki/Kankakee_River "Kankakee River") system. It also spread northward into Indiana along the [Wabash](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River"), [Tippecanoe](/wiki/Tippecanoe_River "Tippecanoe River"), and [White](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)") Rivers.[[14]](#cite_note-Midwestlit-14)

Mississippian peoples in the Midwest were mostly farmers who followed the rich, flat floodplains of Midwestern rivers. They brought with them a well-developed agricultural complex based on three major crops—[maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [beans](/wiki/Beans "Beans"), and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(plant) "Squash (plant)"). Maize, or corn, was the primary crop of Mississippian farmers. They gathered a wide variety of seeds, nuts, and berries, and fished and hunted for fowl to supplement their diets. With such an intensive form of [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), this culture supported large populations.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The Mississippi period was characterized by a [mound-building](/wiki/Mound_builder_(people) "Mound builder (people)") culture. The Mississippians suffered a tremendous population decline about 1400, coinciding with the global climate change of the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"). Their culture effectively ended before 1492.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### Great Lakes Native Americans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Lakes Native Americans")]

The major tribes of the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") region included the [Huron](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people"), [Ottawa](/wiki/Odawa_people "Odawa people"), [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"), [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk"), [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"), [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), [Neutrals](/wiki/Neutral_Nation "Neutral Nation"), and the [Miami](/wiki/Miami_people "Miami people"). Most numerous were the Huron and Ho-Chunk. Fighting and battle were often launched between tribes, with the losers forced to flee.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Most are of the [Algonquian language](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") family. Some tribes—such as the [Stockbridge-Munsee](/wiki/Stockbridge-Munsee_Community "Stockbridge-Munsee Community") and the [Brothertown](/wiki/Brothertown_Indians "Brothertown Indians")—are also Algonkian-speaking tribes who relocated from the eastern seaboard to the Great Lakes region in the 19th century. The [Oneida](/wiki/Oneida_people "Oneida people") belong to the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") language group and the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") of Wisconsin are one of the few Great Lakes tribes to speak a [Siouan](/wiki/Siouan_languages "Siouan languages") language.[[18]](#cite_note-18) American Indians in this area did not develop a written form of language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Winnebago_wigwam.jpg/330px-Winnebago_wigwam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winnebago_wigwam.jpg)

[Winnebago](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") family (1852) - Ho-Chunks are often referred to in historical sources by the derogatory [exonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym"), *Winnebago*

In the 16th century, the natives of the area used projectiles and tools of stone, bone, and wood to hunt and farm. They made [canoes](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe") for fishing. Most of them lived in oval or conical [wigwams](/wiki/Wigwam "Wigwam") that could be easily moved away. Various tribes had different ways of living. The Ojibwe were primarily hunters and fishing was also important in the Ojibwe economy. Other tribes such as Sac, Fox, and Miami, both hunted and farmed.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

They were oriented toward the open prairies where they engaged in communal hunts for [buffalo (bison)](/wiki/American_Bison "American Bison"). In the northern forests, the Ottawas and Potawatomis separated into small family groups for hunting. The Winnebagos and Menominees used both hunting methods interchangeably and built up widespread trade networks extending as far west as the Rockies, north to the Great Lakes, south to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), and east to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").[[20]](#cite_note-20)
The Hurons reckoned descent through the female line, while the others favored the patrilineal method. All tribes were governed under [chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom") or complex chiefdoms. For example, Hurons were divided into matrilineal clans, each represented by a chief in the town council, where they met with a town chief on civic matters. But [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe") people's social and political life was simpler than that of settled tribes.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The religious beliefs varied among tribes. Hurons believed in *Yoscaha*, a supernatural being who lived in the sky and was believed to have created the world and the Huron people. At death, Hurons thought the soul left the body to live in a village in the sky. Ojibwe were a deeply religious people who believed in the Great Spirit. They worshiped the Great Spirit through all their seasonal activities, and viewed religion as a private matter: Each person's relation with his personal guardian spirit was part of his thinking every day of life. Ottawa and Potawatomi people had very similar religious beliefs to those of the Ojibwe.[[14]](#cite_note-Midwestlit-14)

In the Ohio River Valley, the dominant food supply was not hunting but agriculture. There were orchards and fields of crops that were maintained by indigenous women. Corn was their most important crop.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

#### Great Plains Indians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Great Plains Indians")]

Main article: [Plains Indians](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Oglala_girl_in_front_of_a_tipi.jpg/250px-Oglala_girl_in_front_of_a_tipi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oglala_girl_in_front_of_a_tipi.jpg)

Young [Oglala Lakota](/wiki/Oglala_Lakota "Oglala Lakota") girl in front of [tipi](/wiki/Tipi "Tipi") with puppy beside her, probably on or near [Pine Ridge Indian Reservation](/wiki/Pine_Ridge_Indian_Reservation "Pine Ridge Indian Reservation"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Cumulus_Clouds_over_Yellow_Prairie2.jpg/330px-Cumulus_Clouds_over_Yellow_Prairie2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cumulus_Clouds_over_Yellow_Prairie2.jpg)

Cumulus clouds hover above a yellowish prairie at [Badlands National Park](/wiki/Badlands_National_Park "Badlands National Park"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), native lands to the Sioux.

The Plains Indians are the [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") who live on the plains and rolling hills of the Great Plains of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). Their colorful equestrian culture and famous conflicts with settlers and the US Army have made the Plains Indians archetypical in literature and art for American Indians everywhere.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Plains Indians are usually divided into two broad classifications, with some degree of overlap. The first group were fully nomadic, following the vast herds of [buffalo](/wiki/American_bison "American bison"). Some tribes occasionally engaged in agriculture, growing tobacco and corn primarily. These included the [Blackfoot](/wiki/Blackfoot "Blackfoot"), [Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho "Arapaho"), [Assiniboine](/wiki/Assiniboine_people "Assiniboine people"), [Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne "Cheyenne"), [Comanche](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche"), [Crow](/wiki/Crow_Nation "Crow Nation"), [Gros Ventre](/wiki/Gros_Ventres "Gros Ventres"), [Kiowa](/wiki/Kiowa "Kiowa"), [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people"), [Lipan](/wiki/Lipan_Apache_people "Lipan Apache people"), [Plains Apache](/wiki/Plains_Apache "Plains Apache") (or Kiowa Apache), [Plains Cree](/wiki/Plains_Cree_people "Plains Cree people"), [Plains Ojibwe](/wiki/Saulteaux "Saulteaux"), [Sarsi](/wiki/Tsuu_T%27ina_Nation "Tsuu T'ina Nation"), [Shoshone](/wiki/Shoshone "Shoshone"), [Stoney](/wiki/Nakoda_(people) "Nakoda (people)"), and [Tonkawa](/wiki/Tonkawa "Tonkawa").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The second group of Plains Indians (sometimes referred to as Prairie Indians) were the semi-sedentary tribes who, in addition to hunting buffalo, lived in villages and raised crops. These included the [Arikara](/wiki/Arikara "Arikara"), [Hidatsa](/wiki/Hidatsa "Hidatsa"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_tribe "Iowa tribe"), [Kaw (or Kansa)](/wiki/Kaw_(tribe) "Kaw (tribe)"), [Kitsai](/wiki/Kichai_people "Kichai people"), [Mandan](/wiki/Mandan "Mandan"), [Missouria](/wiki/Missouria "Missouria"), [Nez Perce](/wiki/Nez_Perce_tribe "Nez Perce tribe"), [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_(tribe) "Omaha (tribe)"), [Osage](/wiki/Osage_Nation "Osage Nation"), [Otoe](/wiki/Otoe_tribe "Otoe tribe"), [Pawnee](/wiki/Pawnee_people "Pawnee people"), [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca"), [Quapaw](/wiki/Quapaw "Quapaw"), [Santee](/wiki/Dakota_people "Dakota people"), [Wichita](/wiki/Wichita_(tribe) "Wichita (tribe)"), and [Yankton](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The nomadic tribes of the Great Plains survived on [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"); some of their major hunts centered on deer and buffalo. Some tribes are described as part of the "Buffalo Culture" (sometimes called, for the [American bison](/wiki/American_bison "American bison")). Although the Plains Indians hunted other animals, such as [elk](/wiki/Elk "Elk") or [antelope](/wiki/Pronghorn "Pronghorn"), bison was their primary game food source. Bison flesh, hide, and bones from [bison hunting](/wiki/Bison_hunting "Bison hunting") provided the chief source of raw materials for items that Plains Indians made, including food, cups, decorations, crafting tools, knives, and clothing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[24]](#cite_note-wildlife-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Smits94-25)

The tribes followed the bison's seasonal grazing and migration. The Plains Indians lived in [teepees](/wiki/Teepees "Teepees") because they were easily disassembled and allowed the nomadic life of following game. When Spanish horses were obtained, the Plains tribes rapidly integrated them into their daily lives. By the early 18th century, many tribes had fully adopted a [horse culture](/wiki/Horse_culture "Horse culture"). Before their adoption of guns, the Plains Indians hunted with [spears](/wiki/Spear "Spear"), [bows](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)"), and [bows and arrows](/wiki/Bows_and_arrows "Bows and arrows"), and various forms of [clubs](/wiki/Club_(weapon) "Club (weapon)"). The use of horses by the Plains Indians made hunting (and warfare) much easier.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Among the most powerful and dominant tribes were the [Dakota](/wiki/Dakota_people "Dakota people") or [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux"), who occupied large amounts of territory in the Great Plains of the Midwest. The area of the [Great Sioux Nation](/wiki/Great_Sioux_Nation "Great Sioux Nation") spread throughout the South and Midwest, up into the areas of Minnesota and stretching out west into the Rocky Mountains. At the same time, they occupied the heart of prime buffalo range, and also an excellent region for furs they could sell to French and American traders for goods such as guns. The Sioux (Dakota) became the most powerful of the Plains tribes and the greatest threat to American expansion.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The Sioux comprise three major divisions based on Siouan dialect and subculture:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *Isáŋyathi* or *Isáŋathi* ("Knife"): residing in the extreme east of the Dakotas, Minnesota and northern Iowa, and are often referred to as the **Santee** or **Eastern Dakota**.
* *Iháŋktȟuŋwaŋ* and *Iháŋktȟuŋwaŋna* ("Village-at-the-end" and "little village-at-the-end"): residing in the [Minnesota River](/wiki/Minnesota_River "Minnesota River") area, they are considered the middle Sioux, and are often referred to as the **Yankton** and the **Yanktonai**, or, collectively, as the *Wičhíyena* (endonym) or the **Western Dakota** (and have been erroneously classified as *[Nakota](/wiki/Nakota "Nakota")*[[29]](#cite_note-29)).
* *Thítȟuŋwaŋ* or Teton (Prairie Dwellers): the westernmost Sioux, known for their hunting and warrior culture, are often referred to as the **Lakota**.

Today, the Sioux maintain many separate tribal governments scattered across several reservations, communities, and reserves in the Dakotas, Nebraska, Minnesota, and Montana in the United States, as well as [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") and southern [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan") in Canada.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

### European exploration and early settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: European exploration and early settlement")]

#### The Middle Ground theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Middle Ground theory")]

The theory of the middle ground was introduced in Richard White's seminal work: *The Middle Ground: Indians, Empires, and Republics in the Great Lakes Region, 1650–1815* originally published in 1991. White defines the middle ground like so:

> The middle ground is the place in between cultures, peoples, and in between empires and the non state world of villages. It is a place where many of the North American subjects and allies of empires lived. It is the area between the historical foreground of European invasion and occupation and the background of Indian defeat and retreat.

— Richard White, The Middle Ground: Indians, Empires, and Republics in the Great Lakes Region, 1650–1815, p. XXVI

White specifically designates "the lands bordering the rivers flowing into the northern Great Lakes and the lands south of the lakes to the Ohio" as the location of the middle ground.[[30]](#cite_note-30) This includes the modern Midwestern states of Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Wisconsin, and Michigan as well as parts of Canada.

The middle ground was formed on the foundations of mutual accommodation and common meanings established between the French and the Indians that then transformed and degraded as both were steadily lost as the French ceded their influence in the region in the aftermath of [their defeat](/wiki/France_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "France in the Seven Years' War") in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") and the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Major aspects of the middle ground include blended culture, the [fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade"), Native alliances with both the French and British, conflicts and treaties with the United States both [during the Revolutionary War](/wiki/Western_theater_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Western theater of the American Revolutionary War") and [after](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars"),[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) and its ultimate clearing/erasure throughout the nineteenth century.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

#### New France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New France")]

Main article: [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France")

European settlement of the area began in the 17th century following French exploration of the region and became known as [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), including the *[Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country")*. The French period began with the exploration of the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") by [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") in 1534 and ending with their cessation of the majority of their holdings in North America to the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") in the [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

#### Mapping of the Mississippi River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Mapping of the Mississippi River")]

Main articles: [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") and [Louis Joliet](/wiki/Louis_Joliet "Louis Joliet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Marquette_and_jolliet_map_1681.jpg/250px-Marquette_and_jolliet_map_1681.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marquette_and_jolliet_map_1681.jpg)

Map by Marquette and Jolliet drawn on their 1673 expedition, published circa 1681

In 1673 the [Governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France") sent [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette"), a Catholic priest and missionary, and [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet"), a [fur trader](/wiki/Fur_trader "Fur trader"), to map the way to the Northwest Passage to the Pacific. They traveled through Michigan's upper peninsula to the northern tip of Lake Michigan. On canoes, they crossed the massive lake and landed at present-day [Green Bay, Wisconsin](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin"). They entered the Mississippi River on 17 June 1673.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Marquette and Jolliet were the first to map the northern portion of the Mississippi River. They confirmed that it was easy to travel from the St. Lawrence River through the Great Lakes all the way to the Gulf of Mexico by water, that the native peoples who lived along the route were generally friendly, and that the natural resources of the lands in between were extraordinary. New France officials led by LaSalle followed up and erected a 4,000-mile (6,400 km) network of fur trading posts.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Fur trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fur trade")]

Main article: [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Nanfan.svg/330px-Nanfan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nanfan.svg)

Beaver hunting grounds, the basis of the fur trade

The [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") was an integral part of early European and Indian relations. It was the foundation upon which their interactions were built and was a system that would evolve over time.

Goods often traded included guns, clothing, blankets, strouds, cloth, tobacco, silver, and alcohol.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: France")]

Main article: [Louisiana (New France)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg/250px-Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg)

The bell donated by King [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") in 1741 to the French mission at [Kaskaskia, Illinois](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"). It was later called the "Liberty Bell of the West", after it was rung to celebrate U.S. victory in the Revolution

The French and Indian exchange of goods was called an exchange of gifts rather than a trade. These gifts held greater meaning to the relationship between the two than a simple economic exchange because the trade itself was inseparable from the social relations it fostered and the alliance it created.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In the meshed French and Algonquian system of trade, the [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") familial metaphor of a father and his children shaped the political relationship between the French and the Natives in this region. The French, regarded as the metaphoric father, were expected to provide for the needs of the Algonquians and, in return, the Algonquians, the metaphoric children, would be obligated to assist and obey them. Traders (*[voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs")*, *[coureur des bois](/wiki/Coureur_des_bois "Coureur des bois")*) coming into Indian villages facilitated this system of symbolic exchange to establish or maintain alliances and friendships.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Marriage also became an important aspect of the trade in both the Ohio River valley and the French *[pays d'en haut](/wiki/Pays_d%27en_Haut "Pays d'en Haut")* with the temporary closing of the French fur trade from 1690 to 1716 and beyond.[[42]](#cite_note-:2-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) French fur traders were forced to abandon most posts and those remaining in the region became illegal traders who potentially sought these marriages to secure their safety.[[42]](#cite_note-:2-42)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Another benefit for French traders marrying Indian women was that the Indian women were in charge of the processing of the pelts necessary to the fur trade.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Women were integral to the fur trade and their contributions were lauded, so much so that the absence of the involvement of an Indian Woman was once cited as the cause for a trader's failure.[[46]](#cite_note-46) When the French fur trade re-opened in 1716 upon the discovery that their overstock of pelts had been ruined, legal French traders continued to marry Indian women and remain in their villages.[[47]](#cite_note-47) With the growing influence of women in the fur trade also came the increasing demand of cloth which very quickly grew to be the most desired trade good.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Britain")]

Main article: [Indian Reserve (1763)](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)")

[English](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") traders entered the Ohio country as a serious competitor to the French in the fur trade around the 1690s.[[49]](#cite_note-:3-49) English (and later British) traders almost consistently offered the Indians better goods and better rates than the French, with the Indians being able to play that to their advantage, thrusting the French and the British into competition with each other to their own benefit.[[49]](#cite_note-:3-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) The Indian demand for certain kinds of cloth in particular fueled this competition.[[51]](#cite_note-51) This, however, changed following the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") with [Britain's victory](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years' War") over France and the cession of New France to [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain").[[52]](#cite_note-:4-52)

The British attempted to establish a more assertive relationship with the Indians of the *pays d'en haut*, eliminating the practise of gift giving which they now saw as unnecessary.[[52]](#cite_note-:4-52) This, in combination with an underwhelming trade relationship with a surplus of whiskey, increase in prices generally, and a shortage of other goods led to unrest among the Indians that was exacerbated by the decision to significantly reduce the amount of rum being traded, a product that British merchants had been including in the trade for years. This would eventually culminate in [Pontiac's War](/wiki/Pontiac%27s_War "Pontiac's War"), which broke out in 1763.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Following the conflict, the British government was forced to compromise and loosely re-created a trade system that was an echo of the French one.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### American settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: American settlement")]

Main article: [American frontier § New Nation](/wiki/American_frontier#New_Nation "American frontier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Map_of_the_United_States%27_land_claims_and_territorial_cessions_%281782%E2%80%931802%29.png/250px-Map_of_the_United_States%27_land_claims_and_territorial_cessions_%281782%E2%80%931802%29.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_United_States%27_land_claims_and_territorial_cessions_(1782%E2%80%931802).png)

The state cessions that eventually allowed for the creation of the territories northwest and south of the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River")

While French control ended in 1763 after their defeat in the Seven Years' War, most of the several hundred French settlers in small villages along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and its tributaries remained, and were not disturbed by the new British administration. By the terms of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), Spain was given [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)"), the area west of the Mississippi. [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") and [Ste. Genevieve](/wiki/Ste._Genevieve,_Missouri "Ste. Genevieve, Missouri") in Missouri were the main towns, but there was little new settlement. France regained Louisiana from Spain in exchange for [Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany") by the terms of the [Treaty of San Ildefonso](/wiki/Third_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "Third Treaty of San Ildefonso") in 1800. Napoleon had lost interest in re-establishing a [French colonial empire in North America](/wiki/New_France "New France") following the [Haitian Revolution](/wiki/Haitian_Revolution "Haitian Revolution") and together with the fact that France could not effectively defend [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") from a possible British attack, he sold the territory to the United States in the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") of 1803. Meanwhile, the British maintained forts and trading posts in U.S. territory, refusing to give them up until 1796 by the [Jay Treaty](/wiki/Jay_Treaty "Jay Treaty").[[55]](#cite_note-55) American settlement began either via routes over the Appalachian Mountains or through the waterways of the Great Lakes. [Fort Pitt](/wiki/Fort_Pitt_(Pennsylvania) "Fort Pitt (Pennsylvania)") (now [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh")) at the source of the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") became the main base for settlers moving into the Midwest. [Marietta, Ohio](/wiki/Marietta,_Ohio "Marietta, Ohio") in 1787 became the first settlement in Ohio, but not until the defeat of Native American tribes at the [Battle of Fallen Timbers](/wiki/Battle_of_Fallen_Timbers "Battle of Fallen Timbers") in 1794 was large-scale settlement possible. Large numbers also came north from Kentucky into southern Ohio, Indiana and Illinois.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The region's fertile soil produced [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") and vegetables; most farmers were self-sufficient. They cut trees and claimed the land, then sold it to newcomers and then moved further west to repeat the process.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Squatters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Squatters")]

Main articles: [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") and [Squatting in the United States](/wiki/Squatting_in_the_United_States "Squatting in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Northwest-territory-usa-1787.png/250px-Northwest-territory-usa-1787.png)](/wiki/File:Northwest-territory-usa-1787.png)

[Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") 1787

Settlers without legal claims, called "squatters", had been moving into the Midwest for years before 1776. They pushed further and further down the Ohio River during the 1760s and 1770s and sometimes engaged in conflict with the Native Americans.[[58]](#cite_note-:5-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) British officials were outraged. These squatters were characterized by British General [Thomas Gage](/wiki/Thomas_Gage "Thomas Gage") as "too Numerous, too Lawless, and Licentious ever to be restrained", and regarded them as "almost out of Reach of Law and government; Neither the Endeavors of Government, or Fear of Indians has kept them properly within Bounds."[[60]](#cite_note-60) The British had a long-standing goal of establishing a [Native American buffer state](/wiki/Indian_barrier_state "Indian barrier state") in the American Midwest to resist American westward expansion.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

With victory in the American Revolution the new government considered evicting the squatters from areas that were now federally owned public lands.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In 1785, soldiers under General [Josiah Harmar](/wiki/Josiah_Harmar "Josiah Harmar") were sent into the Ohio country to destroy the crops and burn down the homes of any squatters they found living there. But overall the federal policy was to move Indians to western lands (such as the [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") in modern Oklahoma) and allow a very large numbers of farmers to replace a small number of hunters. Congress repeatedly debated how to legalize settlements. On the one hand, Whigs such as [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay") wanted the government to get maximum revenue and also wanted stable middle-class law-abiding settlements of the sort that supported towns (and bankers). Jacksonian Democrats such as [Thomas Hart Benton](/wiki/Thomas_Hart_Benton_(politician) "Thomas Hart Benton (politician)") wanted the support of poor farmers, who reproduced rapidly, had little cash, and were eager to acquire cheap land in the West. Democrats did not want a big government, and keeping revenues low helped that cause. Democrats avoided words like "squatter" and regarded "actual settlers" as those who gained title to land, settled on it, and then improved upon it by building a house, clearing the ground, and planting crops. A number of means facilitated the legal settlement of the territories in the Midwest: [land speculation](/wiki/Speculation "Speculation"), [federal public land auctions](/wiki/Public_domain_(land) "Public domain (land)"), bounty [land grants](/wiki/Land_grant "Land grant") in lieu of pay to military veterans, and, later, [preemption rights](/wiki/Preemption_(land) "Preemption (land)") for squatters. The "squatters" became "pioneers" and were increasingly able to purchase the lands on which they had settled for the minimum price thanks to various preemption acts and laws passed throughout the 1810s-1840s. In Washington, Jacksonian Democrats favored squatter rights while banker-oriented Whigs were opposed; the Democrats prevailed.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

#### Native American wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Native American wars")]

Main article: [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars")

In 1791, General [Arthur St. Clair](/wiki/Arthur_St._Clair "Arthur St. Clair") became commander of the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") and led a [punitive expedition](/wiki/Punitive_expedition "Punitive expedition") with two Regular Army regiments and some militia. Near modern-day [Fort Recovery](/wiki/Fort_Recovery "Fort Recovery"), his force advanced to the location of Native American settlements near the headwaters of the [Wabash River](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River"), but on November 4 they were routed in battle by a tribal confederation led by [Miami](/wiki/Miami_tribe "Miami tribe") Chief [Little Turtle](/wiki/Little_Turtle "Little Turtle") and Shawnee chief [Blue Jacket](/wiki/Blue_Jacket "Blue Jacket"). More than 600 soldiers and scores of women and children were killed in the battle, which has since borne the name "[St. Clair's Defeat](/wiki/St._Clair%27s_Defeat "St. Clair's Defeat")". It remains the greatest defeat of a U.S. Army by Native Americans.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The British demanded the establishment of a [Native American barrier state](/wiki/Indian_barrier_state "Indian barrier state") at the [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent") which ended the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), but American negotiators rejected the idea because Britain had lost control of the region in the [Battle of Lake Erie](/wiki/Battle_of_Lake_Erie "Battle of Lake Erie") and the [Battle of the Thames](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Thames "Battle of the Thames") in 1813, where [Tecumseh](/wiki/Tecumseh "Tecumseh") was killed by U.S. forces. The British then abandoned their Native American allies south of the lakes. The Native Americans ended being the main losers in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). Apart from the short [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War") of 1832, the days of Native American warfare east of the Mississippi River had ended.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

#### Lewis and Clark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Lewis and Clark")]

Main article: [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Louisiana_Purchase.png/250px-Louisiana_Purchase.png)](/wiki/File:Louisiana_Purchase.png)

[Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") 1803

In 1803, President [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") commissioned the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") that took place between May 1804 and September 1806. Launching from [Camp Dubois](/wiki/Camp_Dubois "Camp Dubois") in [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), the goal was to explore the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), and establish trade and U.S. sovereignty over the native peoples along the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"). The Lewis and Clark Expedition established relations with more than two dozen indigenous nations west of the Missouri River.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
The Expedition returned east to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") in the spring of 1806.

### Party politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Party politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg/250px-LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg)

The first local meeting of the new Republican Party took place at the [Little White Schoolhouse](/wiki/Little_White_Schoolhouse "Little White Schoolhouse") in [Ripon, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ripon,_Wisconsin "Ripon, Wisconsin") on March 20, 1854.

The Midwest has been a key swing district in national elections, with highly contested elections in closely divided states often deciding the national result. From 1860 to 1920, both parties tried to find their presidential and vice presidential candidates from the region.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

One of the two major political parties in the United States, the [Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "History of the Republican Party (United States)"), originated in the Midwest in the 1850s; [Ripon, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ripon,_Wisconsin "Ripon, Wisconsin"), had the first local meeting while [Jackson, Michigan](/wiki/Jackson,_Michigan "Jackson, Michigan"), had the first statewide meeting of the new party. Its membership included many [Yankees](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee") out of New England and New York who had settled the upper Midwest. The party opposed the expansion of slavery and stressed the Protestant ideals of thrift, a hard work ethic, self-reliance, democratic decision making, and religious tolerance.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In the early 1890s, the wheat-growing regions were strongholds of the short-lived [Populist movement](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(United_States) "People's Party (United States)") in the Plains states.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Starting in the 1890s, the middle class urban [Progressive movement](/wiki/Progressive_movement "Progressive movement") became influential in the region (as it was in other regions), with Wisconsin a major center. Under the [La Follettes](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette,_Sr. "Robert M. La Follette, Sr."), Wisconsin fought against the Republican bosses and for efficiency, modernization, and the use of experts to solve social, economic, and political problems.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Theodore Roosevelt's [1912 Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1912) "Progressive Party (United States, 1912)") had the best showing in this region, carrying the states of Michigan, Minnesota, and South Dakota. In 1924, La Follette, Sr.'s [1924 Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1924%E2%80%9334) "Progressive Party (United States, 1924–34)") did well in the region, but carried only his home base of Wisconsin.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The Midwest—especially the areas west of Chicago—has always been a stronghold of [isolationism](/wiki/United_States_non-interventionism "United States non-interventionism"), a belief that America should not involve itself in foreign entanglements. This position was largely based on the many [German American](/wiki/German_American "German American") and [Swedish-American](/wiki/Swedish-American "Swedish-American") communities. Isolationist leaders included the La Follettes, Ohio's [Robert A. Taft](/wiki/Robert_A._Taft "Robert A. Taft"), and [Colonel Robert McCormick](/wiki/Robert_R._McCormick "Robert R. McCormick"), publisher of the *Chicago Tribune*.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)

#### Yankees and ethnocultural politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Yankees and ethnocultural politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Rome_Waterfront%2C_on_the_Ohio_River.jpg/250px-Rome_Waterfront%2C_on_the_Ohio_River.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rome_Waterfront,_on_the_Ohio_River.jpg)

[Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") near [Rome, Ohio](/wiki/Rome,_Ohio "Rome, Ohio")

Main article: [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory")

Yankee settlers from New England started arriving in Ohio before 1800, and spread throughout the northern half of the Midwest. Most of them started as farmers, but later the larger proportion moved to towns and cities as entrepreneurs, businessmen, and urban professionals. Since its beginnings in the 1830s, Chicago has grown to dominate the Midwestern metropolis landscape for over a century.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Historian John Bunker has examined the worldview of the Yankee settlers in the Midwest:

> Because they arrived first and had a strong sense of community and mission, Yankees were able to transplant New England institutions, values, and mores, altered only by the conditions of frontier life. They established a public culture that emphasized the work ethic, the sanctity of private property, individual responsibility, faith in residential and social mobility, practicality, piety, public order and decorum, reverence for public education, activists, honest and frugal government, town meeting democracy, and he believed that there was a public interest that transcends particular and stick ambitions. Regarding themselves as the elect and just in a world rife with sin, air, and corruption, they felt a strong moral obligation to define and enforce standards of community and personal behavior....This pietistic worldview was substantially shared by British, Scandinavian, Swiss, English-Canadian and Dutch Reformed immigrants, as well as by German Protestants and many of the [Forty-Eighters](/wiki/Forty-Eighters "Forty-Eighters").[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Midwestern politics pitted Yankees against the German Catholics and Lutherans, who were often led by the Irish Catholics. These large groups, Buenker argues:

> Generally subscribed to the work ethic, a strong sense of community, and activist government, but were less committed to economic individualism and privatism and ferociously opposed to government supervision of the personal habits. Southern and eastern European immigrants generally leaned more toward the Germanic view of things, while modernization, industrialization, and urbanization modified nearly everyone's sense of individual economic responsibility and put a premium on organization, political involvement, and education.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Development of transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Development of transportation")]

#### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Waterways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Indiana_Dunes_National_Lakeshore%2C_Michigan_City%2C_Indiana%2C_Estados_Unidos%2C_2012-10-20%2C_DD_03.jpg/250px-Indiana_Dunes_National_Lakeshore%2C_Michigan_City%2C_Indiana%2C_Estados_Unidos%2C_2012-10-20%2C_DD_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indiana_Dunes_National_Lakeshore,_Michigan_City,_Indiana,_Estados_Unidos,_2012-10-20,_DD_03.jpg)

[Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") is shared by Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, and Wisconsin. Pictured is [Indiana Dunes National Park](/wiki/Indiana_Dunes_National_Park "Indiana Dunes National Park") in northwest Indiana.

Three waterways have been important to the development of the Midwest. The first and foremost was the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), which flowed into the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). Development of the region was halted until 1795 by Spain's control of the southern part of the Mississippi and its refusal to allow the shipment of American crops down the river and into the Atlantic Ocean.[[85]](#cite_note-:0-85) This was changed with the 1795 signing of [Pinckney's Treaty](/wiki/Pinckney%27s_Treaty "Pinckney's Treaty").[[85]](#cite_note-:0-85)

The second waterway is the network of routes within the Great Lakes. The opening of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") in 1825 completed an all-water shipping route, more direct than the Mississippi, to [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and the seaport of New York City. In 1848, The [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal") breached the [continental divide](/wiki/Continental_divide "Continental divide") spanning the [Chicago Portage](/wiki/Chicago_Portage "Chicago Portage") and linking the waters of the Great Lakes with those of the [Mississippi Valley](/wiki/Mississippi_Valley "Mississippi Valley") and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). Lakeport and river cities grew up to handle these new shipping routes. During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the lakes became a conduit for [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") from the [Mesabi Range](/wiki/Mesabi_Range "Mesabi Range") of Minnesota to [steel mills](/wiki/Steel_mill "Steel mill") in the [Mid-Atlantic States](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States"). The [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway"), completed in 1959, opened the Midwest to the Atlantic Ocean.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

The third waterway, the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"), extended water travel from the Mississippi almost to the Rocky Mountains.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1870s and 1880s, the Mississippi River inspired two classic books—*[Life on the Mississippi](/wiki/Life_on_the_Mississippi "Life on the Mississippi")* and *[Adventures of Huckleberry Finn](/wiki/Adventures_of_Huckleberry_Finn "Adventures of Huckleberry Finn")*—written by native Missourian Samuel Clemens, who used the pseudonym [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain"). His stories became staples of Midwestern lore. Twain's hometown of [Hannibal, Missouri](/wiki/Hannibal,_Missouri "Hannibal, Missouri"), is a tourist attraction offering a glimpse into the Midwest of his time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Inland canals in Ohio and Indiana constituted another important waterway, which connected with Great Lakes and Ohio River traffic. The commodities that the Midwest funneled into the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") down the Ohio River contributed to the wealth of New York City, which overtook [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

#### Railroads and the automobile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Railroads and the automobile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/00DI0943_-_Flickr_-_USDAgov.jpg/250px-00DI0943_-_Flickr_-_USDAgov.jpg)](/wiki/File:00DI0943_-_Flickr_-_USDAgov.jpg)

[Homesteaders](/wiki/Homestead_Acts "Homestead Acts") in central Nebraska in 1888

During the mid-19th century, the region got its first railroads, and the railroad junction in Chicago became the world's largest. During the century, Chicago became the nation's railroad center. By 1910, over 20 railroads operated passenger service out of six different downtown terminals. Even today, a century after [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford"), six [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad") ([Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific "Union Pacific"), [BNSF](/wiki/BNSF "BNSF"), [Norfolk Southern](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern "Norfolk Southern"), [CSX](/wiki/CSX "CSX"), [Canadian National](/wiki/Canadian_National "Canadian National"), and [Canadian Pacific](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific "Canadian Pacific")) meet in Chicago.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECondit197343–49,_58,_318–319-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

In the period from 1890 to 1930, many Midwestern cities were connected by electric [interurban](/wiki/Interurban "Interurban") railroads, similar to streetcars. The Midwest had more interurbans than any other region. In 1916, Ohio led all states with 2,798 miles (4,503 km), Indiana followed with 1,825 miles (2,937 km). These two states alone had almost a third of the country's interurban trackage.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The nation's largest interurban junction was in Indianapolis. During the decade of the early 1900s, that city's 38 percent growth in population was attributed largely to the interurban.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Competition with automobiles and buses undermined the interurban and other railroad passenger business. By 1900, [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit,_Michigan "Detroit, Michigan") was the world center of the auto industry, and soon practically every city within 200 miles (320 km) was producing auto parts that fed into its giant factories.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In 1903, Henry Ford founded the [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"). Ford's manufacturing—and those of automotive pioneers [William C. Durant](/wiki/William_C._Durant "William C. Durant"), the [Dodge](/wiki/Dodge "Dodge") brothers, [Packard](/wiki/Packard "Packard"), and [Walter Chrysler](/wiki/Walter_Chrysler "Walter Chrysler")—established Detroit's status in the early 20th century as the world's automotive capital. The proliferation of businesses created a synergy that also encouraged truck manufacturers such as Rapid and [Grabowsky](/wiki/GMC_(automobile) "GMC (automobile)").[[93]](#cite_note-Woodford-93)

The growth of the auto industry was reflected by changes in businesses throughout the Midwest and nation, with the development of garages to service vehicles and gas stations, as well as factories for parts and tires. Today, greater Detroit remains home to [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler"), and the Ford Motor Company.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### American Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: American Civil War")]

Main article: [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")

#### Slavery prohibition and the Underground Railroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Slavery prohibition and the Underground Railroad")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/US_Slave_Free_1789-1861.gif/500px-US_Slave_Free_1789-1861.gif)](/wiki/File:US_Slave_Free_1789-1861.gif)

An animation depicting when United States territories and states forbade or allowed slavery, 1789–1861

The Northwest Ordinance region, comprising the heart of the Midwest, was the first large region of the United States that prohibited [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") (the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") [emancipated](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") slaves into the 1830s). The regional southern boundary was the Ohio River, the border of freedom and slavery in American history and literature (see *[Uncle Tom's Cabin](/wiki/Uncle_Tom%27s_Cabin "Uncle Tom's Cabin")* by [Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe") and *[Beloved](/wiki/Beloved_(novel) "Beloved (novel)")* by [Toni Morrison](/wiki/Toni_Morrison "Toni Morrison")).

The Midwest, particularly Ohio, provided the primary routes for the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"), whereby Midwesterners assisted slaves to freedom from their crossing of the Ohio River through their departure on [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") to Canada. Created in the early 19th century, the Underground Railroad was at its height between 1850 and 1860. One estimate suggests that by 1850, 100,000 slaves had escaped via the Underground Railroad.[[94]](#cite_note-afroamhistory-94)

The Underground Railroad consisted of meeting points, secret routes, transportation, and safe houses and assistance provided by abolitionist sympathizers. Individuals were often organized in small, independent groups; this helped to maintain secrecy because individuals knew some connecting "stations" along the route, but knew few details of their immediate area. Escaped slaves would move north along the route from one way station to the next. Although the fugitives sometimes traveled on boat or train, they usually traveled on foot or by wagon.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

The region was shaped by the relative absence of slavery (except for Missouri), pioneer settlement, education in [one-room free public schools](/wiki/One-room_school "One-room school"), democratic notions brought by [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") veterans, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") faiths and experimentation, and agricultural wealth transported on the Ohio River [riverboats](/wiki/Riverboat "Riverboat"), [flatboats](/wiki/Flatboat "Flatboat"), [canal boats](/wiki/Barge "Barge"), and [railroads](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Bleeding Kansas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Bleeding Kansas")]

Main article: [Bleeding Kansas](/wiki/Bleeding_Kansas "Bleeding Kansas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/The_Tragic_Prelude_John_Brown.jpg/250px-The_Tragic_Prelude_John_Brown.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Tragic_Prelude_John_Brown.jpg)

*[Tragic Prelude](/wiki/Tragic_Prelude "Tragic Prelude")*, in the [Kansas State Capitol](/wiki/Kansas_State_Capitol "Kansas State Capitol")

The first violent conflicts leading up to the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") occurred between two neighboring Midwestern states, Kansas and Missouri, involving [anti-slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") [Free-Staters](/wiki/Free-Stater_(Kansas) "Free-Stater (Kansas)") and pro-slavery "[Border Ruffian](/wiki/Border_Ruffian "Border Ruffian")" elements, that took place in the [Kansas Territory](/wiki/Kansas_Territory "Kansas Territory") and the western frontier towns of Missouri roughly between 1854 and 1858. At the heart of the conflict was the question of whether Kansas would enter the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") as a free state or slave state. As such, [Bleeding Kansas](/wiki/Bleeding_Kansas "Bleeding Kansas") was a [proxy war](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") between [Northerners](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") and [Southerners](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") over the issue of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"). The term "Bleeding Kansas" was coined by [Horace Greeley](/wiki/Horace_Greeley "Horace Greeley") of the *[New-York Tribune](/wiki/New-York_Tribune "New-York Tribune")*.

The immediate cause of the events was the [Kansas–Nebraska Act of 1854](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act"). The Act created the territories of Kansas and Nebraska, opened new lands that would help settlement in them, repealed the [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise_of_1820 "Missouri Compromise of 1820"), and allowed settlers in those territories to determine through [popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Popular sovereignty in the United States") whether to allow slavery within their boundaries. It was hoped the Act would ease relations between the North and the South, because the South could expand slavery to new territories, but the North still had the right to abolish slavery in its states. Instead, opponents denounced the law as a concession to the [slave power](/wiki/The_Slave_Power "The Slave Power") of the South.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Undergroundrailroadsmall2.jpg/500px-Undergroundrailroadsmall2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Undergroundrailroadsmall2.jpg)

A map of various [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad") routes

An ostensibly [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") idea, popular sovereignty stated that the inhabitants of each territory or state should decide whether it would be a free or slave state; however, this resulted in immigration *en masse* to Kansas by activists from both sides. At one point, Kansas had two separate governments, each with its own constitution, although only one was federally recognized. On January 29, 1861, Kansas was admitted to the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") as a free state, less than three months before the [Battle of Fort Sumter](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Sumter "Battle of Fort Sumter") officially began the Civil War.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

On May 21, 1856, the [Free Soil](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party") town of [Lawrence, Kansas](/wiki/Lawrence,_Kansas "Lawrence, Kansas"), was sacked by an armed pro-slavery force from Missouri. A few days later, the [Sacking of Lawrence](/wiki/Sacking_of_Lawrence "Sacking of Lawrence") led [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") [John Brown](/wiki/John_Brown_(abolitionist) "John Brown (abolitionist)") and six of his followers to execute five men along the [Pottawatomie Creek](/wiki/Pottawatomie_massacre "Pottawatomie massacre") in [Franklin County, Kansas](/wiki/Franklin_County,_Kansas "Franklin County, Kansas"), in retaliation.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The so-called "Border War" lasted from May through October between armed bands of pro-slavery and Free Soil men. The U.S. Army had two garrisons in Kansas, the First Cavalry Regiment at [Fort Leavenworth](/wiki/Fort_Leavenworth "Fort Leavenworth") and the [Second Dragoons](/wiki/2nd_Stryker_Cavalry_Regiment_(United_States) "2nd Stryker Cavalry Regiment (United States)") and Sixth Infantry at [Fort Riley](/wiki/Fort_Riley "Fort Riley").[[99]](#cite_note-99) The skirmishes endured until a new governor, John W. Geary, managed to prevail upon the Missourians to return home in late 1856.

National reaction to the events in Kansas demonstrated how deeply divided the country had become. The Border Ruffians were widely applauded in the South, even though their actions had cost the lives of numerous people. In the North, the murders committed by Brown and his followers were ignored by most, and lauded by a few.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The election of [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") in November 1860 was the final trigger for [secession](/wiki/Secession "Secession") by the Southern states.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

The U.S. federal government was supported by 20 mostly-Northern free states in which slavery already had been abolished, and by five slave states that became known as the [border states](/wiki/Border_states_(American_Civil_War) "Border states (American Civil War)"). All of the Midwestern states but one, Missouri, banned slavery. Though most battles were fought in the South, skirmishes between Kansas and Missouri continued until culmination with the [Lawrence Massacre](/wiki/Lawrence_Massacre "Lawrence Massacre") on August 21, 1863, in which [Quantrill's Raiders](/wiki/Quantrill%27s_Raiders "Quantrill's Raiders") raided and plundered Lawrence, killing more than 150 and burning all the business buildings and most of the dwellings.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

### Immigration and industrialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Immigration and industrialization")]

Main articles: [Immigrants to the United States](/wiki/Immigrants_to_the_United_States "Immigrants to the United States") and [Industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Standard_Oil_Company_1889_CP04381_-_DPLA_-_032e381bb16b1a45abecfd1a6a86ae3a_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Standard_Oil_Company_1889_CP04381_-_DPLA_-_032e381bb16b1a45abecfd1a6a86ae3a_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standard_Oil_Company_1889_CP04381_-_DPLA_-_032e381bb16b1a45abecfd1a6a86ae3a_(cropped).jpg)

The first [Standard Oil](/wiki/Standard_Oil "Standard Oil") refinery was opened in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") by businessman [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI.jpg/250px-TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI.jpg)](/wiki/File:TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI.jpg)

Miners at the [Tamarack mine](/wiki/Tamarack_mine "Tamarack mine") in Michigan's [Copper Country](/wiki/Copper_Country "Copper Country"), 1905

By the time of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), European [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") bypassed the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") to settle directly in the interior: [German immigrants](/wiki/German_American "German American") to Ohio, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Michigan, Indiana, Illinois, Iowa, Nebraska, Kansas, and Missouri; [Irish immigrants](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") to port cities on the Great Lakes, like Cleveland and Chicago; [Danes](/wiki/Danes "Danes"), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs"), [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes "Swedes"), and [Norwegians](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians") to Iowa, Nebraska, Wisconsin, Minnesota, and the [Dakotas](/wiki/Dakotas "Dakotas"); and [Finns](/wiki/Finns "Finns") to [Upper Michigan](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") and northern/central Minnesota and Wisconsin. [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people"), and Jews settled in Midwestern cities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The U.S. was predominantly rural at the time of the Civil War. The Midwest was no exception, dotted with small farms all across the region. The late 19th century saw [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration"), and [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") that fed the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), and the heart of industrial domination and innovation was in the [Great Lakes states](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region_(North_America) "Great Lakes region (North America)") of the Midwest, which only began its slow decline by the late 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A flourishing economy brought residents from rural communities and [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") from abroad. Manufacturing and retail and finance sectors became dominant, influencing the American economy.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

In addition to manufacturing, printing, publishing, and food processing also play major roles in the Midwest's largest economy. Chicago was the base of commercial operations for industrialists [John Crerar](/wiki/John_Crerar_(industrialist) "John Crerar (industrialist)"), [John Whitfield Bunn](/wiki/John_Whitfield_Bunn "John Whitfield Bunn"), [Richard Teller Crane](/wiki/Richard_Teller_Crane "Richard Teller Crane"), [Marshall Field](/wiki/Marshall_Field "Marshall Field"), [John Farwell](/wiki/John_Farwell "John Farwell"), [Julius Rosenwald](/wiki/Julius_Rosenwald "Julius Rosenwald"), and many other commercial visionaries who laid the foundation for Midwestern and global industry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Meanwhile, [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller"), creator of the [Standard Oil](/wiki/Standard_Oil "Standard Oil") Company, made his billions in Cleveland. At one point during the late 19th century, Cleveland was home to more than 50% of the world's millionaires, many living on the famous [Millionaire's Row](/wiki/Millionaire%27s_Row "Millionaire's Row") on Euclid Avenue.

In the 20th century, [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") migration from the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") into the Midwestern states changed Chicago, St. Louis, Cleveland, Milwaukee, Kansas City, Cincinnati, Detroit, Omaha, Minneapolis, and many other cities in the Midwest, as factories and schools enticed families by the thousands to new opportunities. Chicago alone gained hundreds of thousands of black citizens from the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") and the [Second Great Migration](/wiki/Second_Great_Migration_(African_American) "Second Great Migration (African American)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Gateway Arch](/wiki/Gateway_Arch "Gateway Arch") monument in St. Louis, clad in stainless steel and built in the form of a flattened [catenary arch](/wiki/Catenary_arch "Catenary arch"),[[104]](#cite_note-modernsteel.com-104) is the tallest man-made monument in the United States,[[105]](#cite_note-nhlsum-105) and the world's tallest arch.[[105]](#cite_note-nhlsum-105) Built as a monument to the [westward expansion of the United States](/wiki/Westward_expansion_of_the_United_States "Westward expansion of the United States"),[[104]](#cite_note-modernsteel.com-104) it is the centerpiece of the [Gateway Arch National Park](/wiki/Gateway_Arch_National_Park "Gateway Arch National Park"), which was known as the Jefferson National Expansion Memorial until 2018, and has become an internationally famous symbol of St. Louis and the Midwest.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### German Americans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: German Americans")]

Main article: [German American](/wiki/German_American "German American")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Distribution_of_Americans_claiming_German_Ancestry_by_county_in_2018.png/330px-Distribution_of_Americans_claiming_German_Ancestry_by_county_in_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_Americans_claiming_German_Ancestry_by_county_in_2018.png)

Distribution of Americans claiming German Ancestry by county in 2018

As the Midwest opened up to settlement via waterways and rail in the mid-1800s, Germans began to settle there in large numbers. The largest flow of German immigration to America occurred between 1820 and World War I, during which time nearly six million Germans immigrated to the United States. From 1840 to 1880, they were the largest group of immigrants.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

The Midwestern cities of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") were favored destinations of German immigrants. By 1900, the populations of the cities of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio"), Milwaukee, [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), and Cincinnati were all more than 40 percent German American. [Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque,_Iowa "Dubuque, Iowa") and [Davenport, Iowa](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa"), had even larger proportions; in [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha "Omaha"), Nebraska, the proportion of German Americans was 57 percent in 1910. In many other cities of the Midwest, such as [Fort Wayne, Indiana](/wiki/Fort_Wayne,_Indiana "Fort Wayne, Indiana"), German Americans were at least 30 percent of the population.[[107]](#cite_note-Faust-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) Many concentrations acquired distinctive names suggesting their heritage, such as the "[Over-the-Rhine](/wiki/Over-the-Rhine "Over-the-Rhine")" district in Cincinnati and "[German Village](/wiki/German_Village "German Village")" in [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"), Ohio.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

A favorite destination was Milwaukee, known as "the German Athens".
