Radical Germans trained in politics in the old country dominated the city's [Socialists](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Social Democratic Party (United States)"). Skilled workers dominated many crafts, while entrepreneurs created the brewing industry; the most famous brands included [Pabst](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company"), [Schlitz](/wiki/Joseph_Schlitz_Brewing_Company "Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company"), [Miller](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company"), and [Blatz](/wiki/Valentin_Blatz_Brewing_Company "Valentin Blatz Brewing Company").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

While half of German immigrants settled in cities, the other half established farms in the Midwest. From Ohio to the Plains states, a heavy presence persists in rural areas into the 21st century.[[111]](#cite_note-Conzen-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, German Americans showed a high interest in becoming farmers, and keeping their children and grandchildren on the land. Western railroads, with large land grants available to attract farmers, set up agencies in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and other German cities, promising cheap transportation, and sales of farmland on easy terms. For example, the [Santa Fe Railroad](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Railroad "Santa Fe Railroad") hired its own commissioner for immigration, and sold over 300,000 acres (1,200 km2) to German-speaking farmers.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

### Politics 1860s–1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Politics 1860s–1920s")]

The Midwest was a battleground for political and economic issues after the Civil War, with voters splitting along ethnic and religious lines rather than class. The [temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement_in_the_United_States "Temperance movement in the United States"), [Greenback](/wiki/Greenback_Party "Greenback Party"), and [populist](/wiki/Populist_movement_(United_States,_19th_Century) "Populist movement (United States, 19th Century)") movements gained attention in the region, with [pietists](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism") supporting the Republicans and ritualists backing the Democrats. [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") was a major issue in the Midwest, with both the [Women's Christian Temperance Union](/wiki/Woman%27s_Christian_Temperance_Union "Woman's Christian Temperance Union") and the [Anti-Saloon League](/wiki/Anti-Saloon_League "Anti-Saloon League") originating in the region. The [18th Amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") was ratified by most Midwestern state legislatures, but the Midwest also became a center of resistance to Prohibition, with ethnic, urban Catholic and German Lutheran voters supporting repeal while native-born, rural pietistic Protestant Midwesterners opposed it.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)

#### Women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Women")]

The presence of women in the Midwest public stage in the late 19th and early 20th centuries aligned with the growing movements for women's rights and prohibition. Women's activism was often presented as an extension of their domestic cleaning role. Activists at the local and state level used the [Woman's Christian Temperance Union](/wiki/Woman%27s_Christian_Temperance_Union "Woman's Christian Temperance Union")'s crusade against alcohol, as a way to push for the right to vote. Midwestern states began allowing women to vote before the [19th Amendment](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") was passed, and the leader of the campaign for the suffrage amendment was [Carrie Chapman Catt](/wiki/Carrie_Chapman_Catt "Carrie Chapman Catt") from Iowa. The 1970s feminist movement also had Midwestern roots, with [Betty Friedan](/wiki/Betty_Friedan "Betty Friedan") from Illinois writing *The Feminine Mystique* in 1963. Economic necessity and the desire for a career also drove women to work outside the home, and certain occupations such as teaching and nursing became feminized.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

#### Workers and populists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Workers and populists")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Debs_Canton_1918_large.jpg/250px-Debs_Canton_1918_large.jpg)](/wiki/File:Debs_Canton_1918_large.jpg)

[Eugene V. Debs](/wiki/Eugene_V._Debs "Eugene V. Debs") speaking in [Canton, Ohio](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio"), in 1918, being arrested for [sedition](/wiki/Sedition "Sedition") shortly thereafter

The Midwest saw labor unrest and rebellion against the capitalist economic order. Chicago played a large role in the [Great Railroad Strike of 1877](/wiki/Great_Railroad_Strike_of_1877 "Great Railroad Strike of 1877"). Later, in 1886, labor leaders organized a protest meeting at [Haymarket Square](/wiki/Haymarket_Square_(Chicago) "Haymarket Square (Chicago)") in Chicago in 1886, where a bomb was thrown among police and eight anarchists were convicted of conspiracy for murder, an event known as the [Haymarket affair](/wiki/Haymarket_affair "Haymarket affair"). The [Pullman Strike of 1894](/wiki/Pullman_Strike "Pullman Strike") was a shutdown of most rail traffic in the Midwest and West. It turned violent and was broken by federal troops. [Eugene V. Debs](/wiki/Eugene_V._Debs "Eugene V. Debs"), leader of the striking [American Railway Union](/wiki/American_Railway_Union "American Railway Union"), went to prison where he converted to Socialism. His version of socialism appealed to some immigrant groups but was too radical for most Midwesterners.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Farmers distrusted big business and adopted cooperative arrangements, such as those offered by the [Grange](/wiki/National_Grange_of_the_Order_of_Patrons_of_Husbandry "National Grange of the Order of Patrons of Husbandry") in the 1870s or the [Farmers' Alliance](/wiki/Farmers%27_Alliance "Farmers' Alliance") in the 1890s. They wanted cooperatives controlled by farmers to handle farm products, a reduction in rail freight rates, and the coining of silver money to raise prices. The Alliance turned to political action with the creation of the [Populist Party](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(United_States) "People's Party (United States)") in 1892. It had local success in the wheat belt and silver mining areas. This venture as a third party was short-lived and they fused with the Democrats in 1896 and voted for Democrat [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan"). Leftwing rural politics continued in the 20th century in the Dakotas and Minnesota with the [Farmer–Labor Party](/wiki/Farmer%E2%80%93Labor_Party "Farmer–Labor Party")[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### 1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 1920s")]

The second [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") experienced a short surge in the Midwest in the early 1920s, fueled by anti-immigrant and anti-Catholic fears. The KKK in the 1920s was a local membership organization, but its autonomous locals were not coordinated and it had little impact on legislation. Members wanted enforcement of vice laws, especially Prohibition, which many immigrants violated. The Klan reached its peak of visibility in Indiana, where the governor supposedly had connections to the secret group. However, the hundreds of [Indiana Klan](/wiki/Indiana_Klan "Indiana Klan") chapters collapsed overnight due to a scandal implicating the state leader in the [abduction and murder of a young woman](/wiki/Murder_of_Madge_Oberholtzer "Murder of Madge Oberholtzer"). The Klan represented a conformist impulse. Middletown (actually the city of [Muncie, Indiana](/wiki/Muncie,_Indiana "Muncie, Indiana")) was the base for a [pioneering sociological study](/wiki/Middletown_studies "Middletown studies") conducted by [Robert S. Lynd](/wiki/Robert_S._Lynd "Robert S. Lynd"). The book revealed a powerful business class that promoted civic boosterism, patriotism, and straight-ticket voting, while discouraging political activism and dissent.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

#### Progressive Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Progressive Era")]

Main article: [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era")

The negative effects of industrialization triggered the political movement of progressivism, which aimed to address its negative consequences through social reform and government regulation. [Jane Addams](/wiki/Jane_Addams "Jane Addams") and [Ellen Gates Starr](/wiki/Ellen_Gates_Starr "Ellen Gates Starr") pioneered the settlement house outreach to newly arrived immigrants by establishing [Hull House](/wiki/Hull_House "Hull House") in Chicago in 1889. Settlement houses provided social services and played an active role in civic life, helping immigrants prepare for naturalization and campaigning for regulation and services from city government.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Midwestern mayors—especially [Hazen S. Pingree](/wiki/Hazen_S._Pingree "Hazen S. Pingree") and [Tom L. Johnson](/wiki/Tom_L._Johnson "Tom L. Johnson"), led early reforms against boss-dominated municipal politics, while [Samuel M. Jones](/wiki/Samuel_M._Jones "Samuel M. Jones") advocated public ownership of local utilities. [Robert M. La Follette](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette "Robert M. La Follette"), the most famous leader of Midwestern progressivism, began his career by winning election against his state's Republican party in 1900. The machine was temporarily defeated, allowing reformers to launch the "[Wisconsin idea](/wiki/Wisconsin_idea "Wisconsin idea")" of expanded democracy. This idea included major reforms such as direct primaries, campaign finance controls, civil service to replace patronage, restrictions on lobbyists, state income and inheritance taxes, child labor restrictions, pure food, and workmen's compensation laws. La Follette promoted government regulation of railroads, public utilities, factories, and banks. Although La Follette lost influence in the national party in 1912, the Wisconsin reforms became a model for progressivism in other states.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

According to Brian Page and Richard Walker:[[123]](#cite_note-123) The sequence of settlement that helped define the subregions of the Midwest was roughly as follows:

> 1800-20, from Pittsburgh down the Ohio Valley to St. Louis at the junction of the Missouri and Mississippi Rivers;   
> 1820-40, the southern Great Lakes shores, from northern Ohio to Milwaukee;   
> 1840-60, infill of the Prairie heartland across to eastern Iowa and southern Minnesota;  
> post-Civil War, the penetration of the northern woodlands and eastern plains from the central Midwest.

The vast central area of the U.S., into Canada, is a landscape of low, flat to rolling terrain in the [Interior Plains](/wiki/Interior_Plains "Interior Plains"), ideal for farming and growing food. Most of its eastern two-thirds form the [Interior Lowlands](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Interior_United_States "Geography of the Interior United States"). The Lowlands gradually rise westward, from a line passing through eastern Kansas, up to over 5,000 feet (1,500 m) in the unit known as the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"). Most of the Great Plains area is now farmed.[[124]](#cite_note-Remote_Sensing_Tutorial-124)

While these states are for the most part relatively flat, consisting either of plains or of rolling and small hills, there is a measure of geographical variation. In particular, the following areas exhibit a high degree of topographical variety: the eastern Midwest near the foothills of the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"); the [Great Lakes Basin](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Basin "Great Lakes Basin"); the heavily glaciated uplands of the [North Shore of Lake Superior](/wiki/North_Shore_(Lake_Superior) "North Shore (Lake Superior)") in Minnesota, part of the ruggedly volcanic [Canadian Shield](/wiki/Canadian_Shield "Canadian Shield"); the [Ozark Mountains](/wiki/Ozark_Mountains "Ozark Mountains") of southern Missouri; and the deeply eroded [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area") of southwest Wisconsin, southeast Minnesota, northeast Iowa, and northwest Illinois.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

Proceeding westward, the [Appalachian Plateau](/wiki/Appalachian_Plateau "Appalachian Plateau") topography gradually gives way to gently rolling hills, and then (in central Ohio) to flat lands converted principally to farms and urban areas. This is the beginning of the vast Interior Plains of North America. As a result, [prairies](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") cover most of the Great Plains states. Iowa and much of Illinois lie within an area called the [prairie peninsula](/wiki/Prairie_peninsula "Prairie peninsula"), an eastward extension of prairies that borders [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") and mixed forests to the north, and [hardwood](/wiki/Hardwood "Hardwood") [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") forests to the east and south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Geographers subdivide the Interior Plains into the Interior Lowlands and the Great Plains on the basis of elevation. The Lowlands are mostly below 1,500 feet (460 m) above sea level whereas the Great Plains to the west are higher, rising in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") to around 5,000 feet (1,500 m).
The Lowlands, then, are confined to parts of Iowa, Illinois, Indiana, Ohio, Michigan, [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee"), and [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"). Missouri and [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") have regions of Lowlands elevations, contrasting with their Ozark region (within the Interior Highlands). Eastern Ohio's hills are an extension of the Appalachian Plateau.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Interior Plains are largely coincident with the vast [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") Drainage System (other major components are the [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and Ohio Rivers). These rivers have for tens of millions of years been eroding downward into the mostly horizontal sedimentary rocks of [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic"), [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic"), and [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic") ages. The modern Mississippi River system has developed during the Pleistocene Epoch of the Cenozoic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Rainfall decreases from east to west, resulting in different types of prairies, with the [tallgrass prairie](/wiki/Tallgrass_prairie "Tallgrass prairie") in the wetter eastern region, mixed-grass prairie in the central [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), and [shortgrass prairie](/wiki/Shortgrass_prairie "Shortgrass prairie") towards the [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") of the Rockies. Today, these three prairie types largely correspond to the [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize")/[soybean](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean") area, the [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") belt, and the western rangelands, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Much of the [coniferous forests](/wiki/Forest#Types "Forest") of the Upper Midwest were clear-cut in the late 19th century, and mixed [hardwood forests](/wiki/Central_U.S._hardwood_forests "Central U.S. hardwood forests") have become a major component of the new woodlands since then. The majority of the Midwest can now be categorized as [urbanized](/wiki/City "City") areas or pastoral [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") areas.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Definitions")]

"Lake states" and "Plains states" redirect here. For the geographic regions, see [Great Lakes region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") and [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Midwestern_United_States_subdivisions.svg/250px-Midwestern_United_States_subdivisions.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Midwestern_United_States_subdivisions.svg)

Divisions of the Midwest by the U.S. Census Bureau into [East North Central](/wiki/East_North_Central_States "East North Central States") and [West North Central](/wiki/West_North_Central_States "West North Central States"), separated largely by the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River")[[1]](#cite_note-CensusRegionsMap-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Scotts_bluff_national_monument.jpg/250px-Scotts_bluff_national_monument.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scotts_bluff_national_monument.jpg)

[Scotts Bluff National Monument](/wiki/Scotts_Bluff_National_Monument "Scotts Bluff National Monument") in western Nebraska

The first recorded use of the term *Midwestern* to refer to a region of the [central U.S.](/wiki/Central_United_States "Central United States") occurred in 1886; *Midwest* appeared in 1894, and *Midwesterner* in 1916.[[126]](#cite_note-oed.com-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) One of the earliest late-19th-century uses of *Midwest* was in reference to Kansas and Nebraska to indicate that they were the civilized areas of the west.[[11]](#cite_note-Blaser_1990_69-11) The term *Midwestern* has been in use since the 1880s to refer to portions of the central United States. A variant term, *Middle West*, has been used since the 19th century and remains relatively common.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)

Traditional definitions of the Midwest include the Northwest Ordinance *[Old Northwest](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory")* states and many states that were part of the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"). The states of the Old Northwest are also known as [Great Lakes states](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region_(North_America) "Great Lakes region (North America)") and are east-north central in the United States. The Ohio River runs along the southeastern section, and the Mississippi River runs north to south near the center. Many of the Louisiana Purchase states in the west-north central United States are also known as the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") states, and the Missouri River is a major waterway joining with the Mississippi. The Midwest lies north of the [36°30′ parallel](/wiki/Parallel_36%C2%B030%E2%80%B2_north "Parallel 36°30′ north"), which the 1820 [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise "Missouri Compromise") established as the dividing line between future [slave and non-slave states](/wiki/Slave_and_free_states "Slave and free states").[[130]](#cite_note-130)

The Midwest Region is defined by the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") as these 12 states:[[1]](#cite_note-CensusRegionsMap-1)

* **[Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"):** Old Northwest, Mississippi River (Missouri River joins near the state border), Ohio River, and Great Lakes state
* **[Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"):** Old Northwest, Ohio River, and Great Lakes state
* **[Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"):** Louisiana Purchase, Mississippi River, and Missouri River state
* **[Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"):** Louisiana Purchase, Great Plains, and Missouri River state
* **[Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"):** Old Northwest and Great Lakes state
* **[Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"):** Old Northwest, Louisiana Purchase, Mississippi River, part of [Red River Colony](/wiki/Red_River_Colony "Red River Colony") before 1818, Great Lakes state
* **[Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"):** Louisiana Purchase, Mississippi River (Ohio River joins near the state border), Missouri River, and [border](/wiki/Border_states_(American_Civil_War) "Border states (American Civil War)") state
* **[Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"):** Louisiana Purchase, Great Plains, and Missouri River state
* **[North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"):** Louisiana Purchase, part of Red River Colony before 1818, Great Plains, and Missouri River state
* **[Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"):** Old Northwest (Historic [Connecticut Western Reserve](/wiki/Connecticut_Western_Reserve "Connecticut Western Reserve")), Ohio River, and Great Lakes state. The [southeastern](/wiki/Appalachian_Ohio "Appalachian Ohio") part of the state is part of northern [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia")
* **[South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"):** Louisiana Purchase, Great Plains, and Missouri River state
* **[Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"):** Old Northwest, Mississippi River, and Great Lakes state

Various organizations define the Midwest with slightly different groups of states. For example, the [Council of State Governments](/wiki/Council_of_State_Governments "Council of State Governments"), an organization for communication and coordination among state governments, includes in its Midwest regional office eleven states from the above list, omitting Missouri, which is in the CSG South region.[[131]](#cite_note-131) The [Midwest Region](/wiki/Organization_of_the_National_Park_Service#Midwest_Region "Organization of the National Park Service") of the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") consists of these twelve states plus the state of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas").[[132]](#cite_note-132) The [Midwest Archives Conference](/wiki/Midwest_Archives_Conference "Midwest Archives Conference"), a professional archives organization, covers the above twelve states, plus [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky").[[133]](#cite_note-133) A 2023 [Emerson College](/wiki/Emerson_College "Emerson College")/*Middle West Review* poll includes the above twelve states, plus [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)

According to the 2022 American Community Survey, 22.6% of the Midwest's population report [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") ancestry, 10.6% report [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") ancestry, 9.4% report [English](/wiki/English_Americans "English Americans") ancestry, 5.9% report [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") ancestry, 4.8% identify their ancestry as [American](/wiki/American_ancestry "American ancestry"), 4.3% report Polish ancestry, and 2.6% report Norwegian ancestry.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147) The Midwest is home to the largest concentration of [German-Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") within the US, with this group making up over 30% of the population in [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"). In addition to German-Americans, the [upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") is home to a large population of [Scandinavian Americans](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans"). In Minnesota, 11.8% of the population identifies with [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_Americans "Norwegian Americans") ancestry, while 6.4% report [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_Americans "Swedish Americans") ancestry.[[148]](#cite_note-148) In North Dakota, 22% of the population reports Norwegian ancestry, the highest rate in the country.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

While the Midwest historically had a very small [Black](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") population,[[150]](#cite_note-150) this changed in the early 20th century as numerous African Americans left the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") for major urban areas in the North and West, fleeing racial persecution and seeking new economic opportunities in a population movement known as the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)").[[151]](#cite_note-151) This drastically changed the demographics of many Midwestern cities that had previously been almost entirely populated by White Americans, leading to [segregation](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining") and discrimination against the growing Black population, [racial violence](/wiki/Race_riots_in_the_United_States "Race riots in the United States"), and "[white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight")" to [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") areas. From 1910 to 1970, Black Americans increased from 2% of [Chicago's](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") population to 33%, and became almost half of the population in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit").[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153) As a result of the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"), Black Americans currently make up 10% of the Midwest's population, with over 96% being concentrated in urban areas, including major cities like Chicago, Detroit, [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), and [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), as well as medium-sized cities like [Gary](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana") and [Flint](/wiki/Flint,_Michigan "Flint, Michigan").[[154]](#cite_note-154) At the same time, most of the rural Midwest has remained overwhelmingly White, with almost two-thirds of the 1,055 counties in the Midwest being over 95% White.

[Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") is the most populous and racially diverse state in the Midwest,[[155]](#cite_note-155) and out of all 50 states, it is also ranked as the most representative of the overall [demographics of the United States](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_United_States "Demographics of the United States") on several metrics, including religion, race/ethnicity, and urban/rural divide.[[156]](#cite_note-156) Almost half of the Hispanic population in the Midwest resides in Illinois, mostly in the Chicago metro area.

As of 2022, the median household income in the Midwest is $70,283, slightly lower than the national average of $74,755. 12.2% of the region's population lives below the poverty line, including roughly 16% of children under 18, and 10% of seniors over 65. The average household size in the Midwest is 2.4 people[[157]](#cite_note-Census_profile:_Midwest_Region-157)

As of 2022, the median age in the Midwest is 39.2 years, with 22% of the population being under 18, and 18% being over 65. The age distribution in the Midwest broadly matches the US as a whole. In terms of [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex"), 50.3% of the region's population are female, and 49.7% are male. Half of the population over the age of 15 are married, while half are unmarried[[157]](#cite_note-Census_profile:_Midwest_Region-157)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Religion")]

Like the rest of the United States, the Midwest is predominantly [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians").[[158]](#cite_note-158)

The majority of Midwesterners are [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), with rates from 48 percent in Illinois to 63 percent in Iowa.[[159]](#cite_note-159) However, the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") is the single largest denomination, varying between 18 percent and 34 percent of the state populations.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") are prevalent in the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"), especially in Michigan, Minnesota, [the Dakotas](/wiki/The_Dakotas "The Dakotas"), and Wisconsin with their large German and Scandinavian populations.[[162]](#cite_note-162) [Southern Baptists](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention") compose about 15 percent of Missouri's population,[[163]](#cite_note-163) but much smaller percentages in other Midwestern states.

[Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") are collectively practiced by 2 percent of the population, with higher concentrations in major urban areas. 35 percent of Midwesterners attend religious services every week, and 69 percent attend at least a few times a year. People with no religious affiliation make up 22 percent of the Midwest's population.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Farming and agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Farming and agriculture")]

Further information: [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt"), [Wheat production in the United States](/wiki/Wheat_production_in_the_United_States "Wheat production in the United States"), and [History of agriculture in the United States](/wiki/History_of_agriculture_in_the_United_States "History of agriculture in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Pastoral-barn.jpg/250px-Pastoral-barn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pastoral-barn.jpg)

A pastoral [farm](/wiki/Farm "Farm") scene near [Traverse City, Michigan](/wiki/Traverse_City,_Michigan "Traverse City, Michigan"), with a classic American red [barn](/wiki/Barn "Barn")

[Agriculture](/wiki/History_of_agriculture_in_the_United_States "History of agriculture in the United States") is one of the biggest drivers of local economies in the Midwest, accounting for billions of dollars' worth of exports and thousands of jobs. The area consists of some of the richest farming land in the world.[[165]](#cite_note-165) The region's fertile soil combined with the steel plow has made it possible for farmers to produce abundant harvests of grain and cereal crops, including [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [soybeans](/wiki/Soybeans "Soybeans"), [oats](/wiki/Oats "Oats"), and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), to become known today as the nation's "breadbasket".[[166]](#cite_note-166) [Henry A. Wallace](/wiki/Henry_A._Wallace "Henry A. Wallace"), a pioneer of hybrid seeds, declared in 1956 that the Corn Belt developed the "most productive agricultural civilization the world has ever seen".[[167]](#cite_note-167) Today, the U.S. produces 40 percent of the world crop.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

The very dense soil of the Midwest plagued the first settlers who were using wooden [plows](/wiki/Plough "Plough"), which were more suitable for loose forest soil. On the prairie, the plows bounced around and the soil stuck to them. This problem was solved in 1837 by an Illinois [blacksmith](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith") named [John Deere](/wiki/John_Deere_(inventor) "John Deere (inventor)") who developed a [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") moldboard plow that was stronger and cut the roots, making the fertile soils of the prairie ready for farming.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Farms spread from the colonies westward along with the settlers. In cooler regions, wheat was often the crop of choice when lands were newly settled, leading to a "wheat frontier" that moved westward over the course of years. Also very common in the antebellum Midwest was farming corn while raising [hogs](/wiki/Hog_(swine) "Hog (swine)"), complementing each other especially since it was difficult to get grain to market before the canals and railroads. After the "wheat frontier" had passed through an area, more diversified farms including [dairy](/wiki/Dairy_cattle "Dairy cattle") and [beef cattle](/wiki/Beef_cattle "Beef cattle") generally took its place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The introduction and broad adoption of scientific agriculture since the mid-19th century contributed to economic growth in the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Maytag.jpg/250px-Maytag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maytag.jpg)

Central [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") cornfield, part of the [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt")

This development was facilitated by the [Morrill Act](/wiki/Morrill_Act "Morrill Act") and the [Hatch Act of 1887](/wiki/Hatch_Act_of_1887 "Hatch Act of 1887") which established in each state a [land-grant university](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") (with a mission to teach and study agriculture) and a federally funded system of [agricultural experiment stations](/wiki/Agricultural_experiment_station "Agricultural experiment station") and [cooperative extension](/wiki/Cooperative_extension "Cooperative extension") networks which place [extension agents](/wiki/Extension_agent "Extension agent") in each state. [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") became the nation's first designated land-grant institution when the [Iowa Legislature](/wiki/Iowa_Legislature "Iowa Legislature") accepted the provisions of the 1862 Morrill Act on September 11, 1862, making Iowa the first state in the nation to do so.[[169]](#cite_note-point-169) [Soybeans](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean") were not widely cultivated in the United States until the early 1930s, and by 1942, the U.S. became the world's largest soybean producer, partially because of World War II and the "need for domestic sources of fats, oils, and meal". Between 1930 and 1942, the United States' share of world soybean production skyrocketed from 3 percent to 46.5 percent, largely as a result of increase in the Midwest, and by 1969, it had risen to 76 percent.[[170]](#cite_note-170)
Iowa and Illinois rank first and second in the nation in soybean production. In 2012, Iowa produced 14.5 percent, and Illinois produced 13.3 percent of the nation's soybeans.[[171]](#cite_note-ers.usda.gov-171)

The [tallgrass prairie](/wiki/Tallgrass_prairie "Tallgrass prairie") has been converted into one of the most intensive crop producing areas in North America. Less than one tenth of one percent (<0.09%) of the original landcover of the tallgrass prairie biome remains.[[172]](#cite_note-172) States formerly with landcover in native tallgrass prairie such as Iowa, Illinois, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Nebraska, and Missouri have become valued for their highly productive soils.

The [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt") is a region of the Midwest where corn has, since the 1850s, been the predominant crop, replacing the native tall grasses. The "Corn Belt" region is defined typically to include Iowa, Illinois, Indiana, southern Michigan, western Ohio, eastern Nebraska, eastern Kansas, southern Minnesota, and parts of Missouri.[[173]](#cite_note-Hart_1986-173) As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit), the top four corn-producing states were Iowa, Illinois, Nebraska, and Minnesota, together accounting for more than half of the corn grown in the United States.[[174]](#cite_note-usda-174) The Corn Belt also sometimes is defined to include parts of South Dakota, North Dakota, Wisconsin, and Kentucky.[[175]](#cite_note-agcensus-175) The region is characterized by relatively level land and deep, fertile soils, high in organic matter.[[176]](#cite_note-Britannica-176)

Iowa produces the largest corn crop of any state. In 2012, Iowa farmers produced 18.3 percent of the nation's corn, while Illinois produced 15.3 percent.[[171]](#cite_note-ers.usda.gov-171) In 2011, there were 13.7 million harvested acres of corn for grain, producing 2.36 billion bushels, which yielded 172.0 bu/acre, with US$14.5 billion of corn value of production.[[177]](#cite_note-iowaagriculture.gov-177)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Kansas_Summer_Wheat_and_Storm_Panorama.jpg/250px-Kansas_Summer_Wheat_and_Storm_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kansas_Summer_Wheat_and_Storm_Panorama.jpg)

[Wheat production](/wiki/Wheat_production_in_the_United_States "Wheat production in the United States") in [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas")

[Wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") is produced throughout the Midwest and is the principal [cereal](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal") grain in the country. The U.S. is ranked third in production volume of wheat, with almost 58 million tons produced in the 2012–2013 growing season, behind only China and India (the combined production of all European Union nations is larger than China)[[178]](#cite_note-178) The U.S. ranks first in crop export volume; almost 50 percent of total wheat produced is exported.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [U.S. Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") defines eight official classes of wheat: [durum](/wiki/Durum "Durum") wheat, hard red spring wheat, hard red winter wheat, soft red winter wheat, hard white wheat, soft white wheat, unclassed wheat, and mixed wheat.[[179]](#cite_note-179) Winter wheat accounts for 70 to 80 percent of total production in the U.S., with the largest amounts produced in Kansas (10.8 million tons) and North Dakota (9.8 million tons). Of the total wheat produced in the country, 50 percent is exported, valued at US$9 billion.[[180]](#cite_note-voanews.com-180)

Midwestern states also lead the nation in other agricultural commodities, including [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork") (Iowa), [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef") and [veal](/wiki/Veal "Veal") (Nebraska), [dairy](/wiki/Dairy "Dairy") (Wisconsin), and [chicken eggs](/wiki/Chicken_eggs "Chicken eggs") (Iowa).[[171]](#cite_note-ers.usda.gov-171)

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Finance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Chicago_bot.jpg/250px-Chicago_bot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_bot.jpg)

The [Chicago Board of Trade](/wiki/Chicago_Board_of_Trade "Chicago Board of Trade") floor in 1993. It is one of the world's oldest [futures and options exchanges](/wiki/Futures_exchange "Futures exchange").[[181]](#cite_note-181)

[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") is the largest economic and financial center of the Midwest, and has the third largest [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP") in North America—approximately $689 billion, after the regions of New York City and Los Angeles. Chicago was named the fourth most important business center in the world in the MasterCard Worldwide Centers of Commerce Index.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The 2021 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") ranked Chicago as the fourth most competitive city in the country and eleventh in the world, directly behind Paris and Tokyo. The [Chicago Board of Trade](/wiki/Chicago_Board_of_Trade "Chicago Board of Trade") (established 1848) listed the first ever standardized "exchange traded" forward contracts, which were called [futures contracts](/wiki/Futures_contract "Futures contract").[[183]](#cite_note-183) As a world financial center, Chicago is home to major financial and [futures exchanges](/wiki/Futures_exchange "Futures exchange") including the [CME Group](/wiki/CME_Group "CME Group") which owns the [Chicago Mercantile Exchange](/wiki/Chicago_Mercantile_Exchange "Chicago Mercantile Exchange") ("the Merc"), [Chicago Board of Trade](/wiki/Chicago_Board_of_Trade "Chicago Board of Trade") (CBOT), the [New York Mercantile Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Mercantile_Exchange "New York Mercantile Exchange") (NYMEX), the [Dow Jones Indexes](/wiki/Dow_Jones_Indexes "Dow Jones Indexes"), and the Commodities Exchange Inc. (COMEX).[[184]](#cite_note-184) Other major exchanges include the [Chicago Board Options Exchange](/wiki/Chicago_Board_Options_Exchange "Chicago Board Options Exchange") (CBOE), the largest options exchange in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"); and the [Chicago Stock Exchange](/wiki/Chicago_Stock_Exchange "Chicago Stock Exchange"). In addition, Chicago is also home to the headquarters of the [Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Chicago "Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago") (the Seventh District of the Federal Reserve).

Outside of Chicago, many other Midwest cities are host to financial centers as well. Federal Reserve Bank districts are also headquartered in [Cleveland](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Cleveland "Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Kansas_City "Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City"), [Minneapolis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Minneapolis "Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis"), and [St. Louis](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_St._Louis "Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis"). Major United States bank headquarters are located throughout Ohio including [Huntington Bancshares](/wiki/Huntington_Bancshares "Huntington Bancshares") in Columbus, [Fifth Third Bank](/wiki/Fifth_Third_Bank "Fifth Third Bank") in Cincinnati, and [KeyCorp](/wiki/KeyCorp "KeyCorp") in Cleveland. Insurance Companies such as [Elevance Health](/wiki/Elevance_Health "Elevance Health") in Indianapolis, [Nationwide Insurance](/wiki/Nationwide_Insurance "Nationwide Insurance") in Columbus, [American Family Insurance](/wiki/American_Family_Insurance "American Family Insurance") in Madison, Wisconsin, [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") in Omaha, [State Farm Insurance](/wiki/State_Farm_Insurance "State Farm Insurance") in Bloomington, Illinois, [Reinsurance Group of America](/wiki/Reinsurance_Group_of_America "Reinsurance Group of America") in [Chesterfield, Missouri](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Missouri "Chesterfield, Missouri"), [Cincinnati Financial Corporation](/wiki/Cincinnati_Financial_Corporation "Cincinnati Financial Corporation") and [American Modern Insurance Group](/wiki/American_Modern_Insurance_Group "American Modern Insurance Group") of Cincinnati, and [Progressive Insurance](/wiki/Progressive_Insurance "Progressive Insurance") and [Medical Mutual of Ohio](/wiki/Medical_Mutual_of_Ohio "Medical Mutual of Ohio") in Cleveland also spread throughout the Midwest.

### Manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Manufacturing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/U.S._STEEL_PLANT_-_NARA_-_547097_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-U.S._STEEL_PLANT_-_NARA_-_547097_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._STEEL_PLANT_-_NARA_-_547097_(retouched).jpg)

The [Gary Works](/wiki/Gary_Works "Gary Works") of [Gary, Indiana](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana") (seen here in 1973) is the largest integrated steel mill in North America.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

Navigable terrain, waterways, and ports spurred an unprecedented construction of [transportation](/wiki/Transport "Transport") [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") throughout the region. The region is a global leader in advanced manufacturing and research and development, with significant innovations in both production processes and business organization. [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller")'s [Standard Oil](/wiki/Standard_Oil "Standard Oil") set precedents for centralized pricing, uniform distribution, and controlled product standards through Standard Oil, which started as a consolidated refinery in Cleveland. [Cyrus McCormick](/wiki/Cyrus_McCormick "Cyrus McCormick")'s Reaper and other manufacturers of agricultural machinery consolidated into [International Harvester](/wiki/International_Harvester "International Harvester") in Chicago. [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie")'s steel production integrated large-scale open-hearth and [Bessemer processes](/wiki/Bessemer_steel "Bessemer steel") into the world's most efficient and profitable mills. The largest, most comprehensive monopoly in the world, [United States Steel](/wiki/United_States_Steel "United States Steel"), consolidated steel production throughout the region. Many of the world's largest employers began in the Great Lakes region.

Advantages of accessible waterways, highly developed transportation infrastructure, finance, and a prosperous market base makes the region the global leader in automobile production and a global business location. [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford")'s movable assembly line and integrated production set the model and standard for major car manufactures. The Detroit area emerged as the world's automotive center, with facilities throughout the region. [Akron, Ohio](/wiki/Akron,_Ohio "Akron, Ohio") became the global leader in rubber production, driven by the demand for tires. Over 200 million tons of [cargo](/wiki/Cargo "Cargo") are shipped annually through the Great Lakes.[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187)[[188]](#cite_note-188)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Milwaukee_Museum_from_south-west.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Museum_from_south-west.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Museum_from_south-west.jpg)

The [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum") is located on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan").

Following the sociological [Middletown studies](/wiki/Middletown_studies "Middletown studies") of 1929, which were based on [Muncie, Indiana](/wiki/Muncie,_Indiana "Muncie, Indiana"),[[189]](#cite_note-189) commentators took Midwestern cities and the Midwest generally to be "typical" of the United States. Earlier, the rhetorical question *[Will it play in Peoria?](/wiki/Will_it_play_in_Peoria%3F "Will it play in Peoria?")* had become a stock phrase, using [Peoria, Illinois](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois") to signal whether something would appeal to mainstream America.[[190]](#cite_note-Scheetz-190) As of 2010 the Midwest has a higher [employment-to-population ratio](/wiki/Employment-to-population_ratio "Employment-to-population ratio") than the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), or the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States").[[191]](#cite_note-191)

[Euchre](/wiki/Euchre "Euchre"), a trick-taking card game, remains popular in the Midwest and parts of the Upper South, particularly in Michigan, Illinois, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, and Pennsylvania.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Ohio_University_Cutler_Hall.png/250px-Ohio_University_Cutler_Hall.png)](/wiki/File:Ohio_University_Cutler_Hall.png)

[Manasseh Cutler Hall](/wiki/Manasseh_Cutler_Hall "Manasseh Cutler Hall"), constructed by 1816, was the first academic building in the former [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Campus_Spring.jpg/250px-Campus_Spring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campus_Spring.jpg)

The [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") is considered among the most prestigious universities in the US.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

Many Midwestern universities are members of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") (AAU), a bi-national organization founded in Chicago of leading [research universities](/wiki/Research_universities "Research universities"). Of the 69 members from the U.S. and Canada, 17 are located in the Midwest. These include private schools [Case Western Reserve University](/wiki/Case_Western_Reserve_University "Case Western Reserve University"), the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"), [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame"), and [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis") and public institutions, the [University of Illinois Urbana–Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana%E2%80%93Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana–Champaign"), [Indiana University Bloomington](/wiki/Indiana_University_Bloomington "Indiana University Bloomington"), the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa"), the [University of Kansas](/wiki/University_of_Kansas "University of Kansas"), the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University"), the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), the [University of Missouri](/wiki/University_of_Missouri "University of Missouri"), the [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_University "Ohio State University"), [Purdue University](/wiki/Purdue_University "Purdue University"), and the [University of Wisconsin–Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison").[[194]](#cite_note-194)

Other notable major [research-intensive](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States "List of research universities in the United States") public universities include the [University of Cincinnati](/wiki/University_of_Cincinnati "University of Cincinnati"), the [University of Illinois at Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Chicago "University of Illinois at Chicago"), [Indiana University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University Indianapolis"), [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University"), [Kansas State University](/wiki/Kansas_State_University "Kansas State University"), the [University of Nebraska–Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln"), [Ohio University](/wiki/Ohio_University "Ohio University"), [Southern Illinois University](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University "Southern Illinois University"), and [Wayne State University](/wiki/Wayne_State_University "Wayne State University").[[195]](#cite_note-195)

Numerous state university systems have established regional campuses statewide. The numerous state teachers colleges were upgraded into state universities after 1945.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

Other notable private institutions include [Beloit College](/wiki/Beloit_College "Beloit College"), [John Carroll University](/wiki/John_Carroll_University "John Carroll University"), [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)"), [Butler University](/wiki/Butler_University "Butler University"), [Loyola University Chicago](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago"), [DePaul University](/wiki/DePaul_University "DePaul University"), [Creighton University](/wiki/Creighton_University "Creighton University"), [Drake University](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University"), [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University"), [University of Dayton](/wiki/University_of_Dayton "University of Dayton"), and [Xavier University](/wiki/Xavier_University "Xavier University"). Local boosters, usually with a church affiliation, created numerous colleges in the mid-19th century.[[197]](#cite_note-197) In terms of national rankings, the most prominent [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_colleges "Liberal arts colleges") today include [Augustana College](/wiki/Augustana_College_(Illinois) "Augustana College (Illinois)"), [Carleton College](/wiki/Carleton_College "Carleton College"), [Denison University](/wiki/Denison_University "Denison University"), [DePauw University](/wiki/DePauw_University "DePauw University"), [Earlham College](/wiki/Earlham_College "Earlham College"), [Grinnell College](/wiki/Grinnell_College "Grinnell College"), [Hamline University](/wiki/Hamline_University "Hamline University"), [Kalamazoo College](/wiki/Kalamazoo_College "Kalamazoo College"), [Kenyon College](/wiki/Kenyon_College "Kenyon College"), [Knox College](/wiki/Knox_College_(Illinois) "Knox College (Illinois)"), [Macalester College](/wiki/Macalester_College "Macalester College"), [Lawrence University](/wiki/Lawrence_University "Lawrence University"), [Oberlin College](/wiki/Oberlin_College "Oberlin College"), [St. Olaf College](/wiki/St._Olaf_College "St. Olaf College"), [College of Saint Benedict and Saint John's University](/wiki/College_of_Saint_Benedict_and_Saint_John%27s_University "College of Saint Benedict and Saint John's University"), [Mount Union University](/wiki/Mount_Union_University "Mount Union University"), [Wabash College](/wiki/Wabash_College "Wabash College"), [Wheaton College](/wiki/Wheaton_College_(Illinois) "Wheaton College (Illinois)"), and [The College of Wooster](/wiki/The_College_of_Wooster "The College of Wooster").[[198]](#cite_note-198)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Health")]

The rate of potentially preventable hospitalizations in the Midwestern United States fell from 2005 to 2011 for overall conditions, acute conditions, and chronic conditions.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

### Linguistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Linguistics")]

Main articles: [Inland Northern American English](/wiki/Inland_Northern_American_English "Inland Northern American English"), [North Central American English](/wiki/North_Central_American_English "North Central American English"), [Yooper dialect](/wiki/Yooper_dialect "Yooper dialect"), and [Midland American English](/wiki/Midland_American_English "Midland American English")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Over-the-Rhine_near_Findlay_Market.jpg/250px-Over-the-Rhine_near_Findlay_Market.jpg)](/wiki/File:Over-the-Rhine_near_Findlay_Market.jpg)

[Over-the-Rhine](/wiki/Over-the-Rhine "Over-the-Rhine") in [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati")

The accents of the region are generally distinct from those of the [American South](/wiki/American_South "American South") and of the urban areas of the [American Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). To a lesser degree, they are also distinct from the accent of the [American West](/wiki/American_West "American West").[[200]](#cite_note-200)

The accent characteristic of most of the Midwest is popularly considered to be that of "standard" American English or [General American](/wiki/General_American "General American"). This accent is typically preferred by many national radio and television producers. Linguist Thomas Bonfiglio argues that, "American English pronunciation standardized as 'network standard' or, informally, 'Midwestern' in the 20th century." He identifies radio as the chief factor.[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-Media_preference-202)

Currently, many cities in the Great Lakes region are undergoing the [Northern Cities Vowel Shift](/wiki/Northern_Cities_Vowel_Shift "Northern Cities Vowel Shift") away from the standard pronunciation of vowels.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

The dialect of Minnesota, western Wisconsin, much of North Dakota and Michigan's Upper Peninsula is referred to as the [Upper Midwestern Dialect](/wiki/North_Central_American_English "North Central American English") (or "Minnesotan"), and has [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and [Canadian](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") influences.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Missouri has elements of three dialects, specifically: [Northern Midland](/wiki/Midland_American_English "Midland American English"), in the extreme northern part of the state, with a distinctive variation in St. Louis and the surrounding area; Southern Midland, in the majority of the state; and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_American_English "Southern American English"), in the southwestern and southeastern parts of the state, with a bulge extending north in the central part, to include approximately the southern one-third.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Hitsville_USA.jpg/250px-Hitsville_USA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitsville_USA.jpg)

The [Hitsville U.S.A.](/wiki/Hitsville_U.S.A. "Hitsville U.S.A.") building in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") was the first headquarters and studio of [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown"), which played an important role in the [racial integration](/wiki/Racial_integration "Racial integration") of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music").

The heavy German immigration played a major role in establishing musical traditions, especially choral and orchestral music.[[205]](#cite_note-205) Czech and German traditions combined to sponsor the polka.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

The Southern Diaspora of the 20th century saw more than twenty million Southerners [move throughout the country](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"), many of whom moved into major Midwestern industrial cities such as Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, and St. Louis.[[207]](#cite_note-207) Along with them, they brought jazz to the Midwest, as well as [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music"), and [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), with major contributions to [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), and even new subgenres such as the [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown") Sound and [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") from Detroit[[208]](#cite_note-208) or [house music](/wiki/House_music "House music") from Chicago. In the 1920s, South Side Chicago was the base for [Jelly Roll Morton](/wiki/Jelly_Roll_Morton "Jelly Roll Morton") (1890–1941). Kansas City developed [its own jazz style](/wiki/Kansas_City_jazz "Kansas City jazz").[[209]](#cite_note-209)

The electrified [Chicago blues](/wiki/Chicago_blues "Chicago blues") sound exemplifies the genre, as popularized by record labels [Chess](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") and [Alligator](/wiki/Alligator_Records "Alligator Records") and portrayed in film *[The Blues Brothers](/wiki/The_Blues_Brothers_(film) "The Blues Brothers (film)")*.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

[Rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") music was first identified as a new genre in 1951 by [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") [disc jockey](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") who began playing this music style while popularizing the term "rock and roll" to describe it.[[211]](#cite_note-Turning_Points-211) By the mid-1950s, rock and roll emerged as a defined musical style in the United States, deriving most directly from the [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") music of the 1940s, which itself developed from earlier [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [boogie woogie](/wiki/Boogie_woogie "Boogie woogie"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), and [swing music](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), and was also influenced by [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [country and western](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), and traditional [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"). Freed's contribution in identifying rock as a new genre helped establish the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame"), located in Cleveland. [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry"), a Midwesterner from St. Louis, influenced many other rock musicians.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Kansas_city_%2816778782291%29.jpg/250px-Kansas_city_%2816778782291%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kansas_city_(16778782291).jpg)

[Downtown Kansas City](/wiki/Downtown_Kansas_City "Downtown Kansas City") looking over [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Kansas_City) "Union Station (Kansas City)")

Since the founding of rock 'n' roll music, an uncountable number of rock, soul, R&B, hip-hop, dance, blues, and jazz acts have emerged from Chicago onto the global and national music scene. Detroit has greatly contributed to the international music scene as a result of being the original home of the legendary [Motown Records](/wiki/Motown_Records "Motown Records"). Notable soul and R&B musicians associated with Motown that had their origins in the area include [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), [the Supremes](/wiki/The_Supremes "The Supremes"), [Mary Wells](/wiki/Mary_Wells "Mary Wells"), [Four Tops](/wiki/Four_Tops "Four Tops"), [the Jackson 5](/wiki/The_Jackson_5 "The Jackson 5"), [Smokey Robinson & the Miracles](/wiki/The_Miracles "The Miracles"), [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), [the Marvelettes](/wiki/The_Marvelettes "The Marvelettes"), [the Temptations](/wiki/The_Temptations "The Temptations"), and [Martha and the Vandellas](/wiki/Martha_and_the_Vandellas "Martha and the Vandellas"). These artists achieved their greatest success in the 1960s and 1970s.

Midwest music fans loved country music, [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [arena rock](/wiki/Arena_rock "Arena rock"), [heartland rock](/wiki/Heartland_rock "Heartland rock"), and TOP 40. In the 1970s and 1980s, native Midwestern musicians such as [Bob Seger](/wiki/Bob_Seger "Bob Seger"), [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp") and [Warren Zevon](/wiki/Warren_Zevon "Warren Zevon") found great success with a style of rock music that came to be known as [heartland rock](/wiki/Heartland_rock "Heartland rock"), characterized by lyrical themes that focused on and appealed to the Midwestern working class. Other successful Midwestern rock artists emerged during this time, including [REO Speedwagon](/wiki/REO_Speedwagon "REO Speedwagon") (Illinois), [Styx](/wiki/Styx_(band) "Styx (band)") (Illinois), and [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_(band) "Kansas (band)").

[Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)"), [The Time](/wiki/The_Time_(band) "The Time (band)"), [Morris Day](/wiki/Morris_Day "Morris Day"), [Jesse Johnson](/wiki/Jesse_Johnson_(musician) "Jesse Johnson (musician)"), [Alexander O'Neal](/wiki/Alexander_O%27Neal "Alexander O'Neal"), [The Family](/wiki/The_Family_(band) "The Family (band)") (USA), St. Paul ([Paul Peterson](/wiki/Paul_Peterson "Paul Peterson")), [Apollonia 6](/wiki/Apollonia_6 "Apollonia 6"), [Vanity 6](/wiki/Vanity_6 "Vanity 6"), Sheila E., and [Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis](/wiki/Jimmy_Jam_and_Terry_Lewis "Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis") recorded [Minneapolis sound](/wiki/Minneapolis_sound "Minneapolis sound").[[213]](#cite_note-213)

[House Music](/wiki/House_Music "House Music"), the first form of [Electronic Dance Music](/wiki/Electronic_Dance_Music "Electronic Dance Music"), had its beginning in Chicago in the early 1980s, and by the late 1980s and the early 1990s house music had become popular on an international scale. House artists such as [Frankie Knuckles](/wiki/Frankie_Knuckles "Frankie Knuckles"), [Marshall Jefferson](/wiki/Marshall_Jefferson "Marshall Jefferson") released many house music records. With the creation of house music in the city of Chicago, the first form of the globally popular electronic dance music genre was created. [Techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") had its start in Detroit in the late 1980s and early 1990s with techno pioneers such as [Juan Atkins](/wiki/Juan_Atkins "Juan Atkins"), [Derrick May](/wiki/Derrick_May_(musician) "Derrick May (musician)"), and [Kevin Saunderson](/wiki/Kevin_Saunderson "Kevin Saunderson"). The genre, while popular in America, became much more popular overseas such as in Europe.[[214]](#cite_note-214)

Numerous [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") [composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") live and have lived in midwestern states, including [Easley Blackwood](/wiki/Easley_Blackwood,_Jr. "Easley Blackwood, Jr."), [Kenneth Gaburo](/wiki/Kenneth_Gaburo "Kenneth Gaburo"), [Salvatore Martirano](/wiki/Salvatore_Martirano "Salvatore Martirano"), and [Ralph Shapey](/wiki/Ralph_Shapey "Ralph Shapey") (Illinois); [Glenn Miller](/wiki/Glenn_Miller "Glenn Miller") and [Meredith Willson](/wiki/Meredith_Willson "Meredith Willson") (Iowa); [Leslie Bassett](/wiki/Leslie_Bassett "Leslie Bassett"), [William Bolcom](/wiki/William_Bolcom "William Bolcom"), [Michael Daugherty](/wiki/Michael_Daugherty "Michael Daugherty"), and [David Gillingham](/wiki/David_Gillingham "David Gillingham") (Michigan); [Donald Erb](/wiki/Donald_Erb "Donald Erb") (Ohio); [Dominick Argento](/wiki/Dominick_Argento "Dominick Argento") and [Stephen Paulus](/wiki/Stephen_Paulus "Stephen Paulus") (Minnesota).

### Sports

The [Milwaukee Mile](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mile "Milwaukee Mile") hosted its first automobile race in 1903, and is one of the oldest tracks in the world, though as of 2019 is presently inactive. The [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway"), opened in 1909, is a prestigious auto racing track which annually hosts the internationally famous [Indianapolis 500-Mile Race](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500") (part of the [IndyCar series](/wiki/IndyCar_series "IndyCar series")), the [Brickyard 400](/wiki/Brickyard_400 "Brickyard 400") ([NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR")), and the [IndyCar Grand Prix](/wiki/IndyCar_Grand_Prix "IndyCar Grand Prix") (IndyCar series). The [Road America](/wiki/Road_America "Road America") and [Mid-Ohio](/wiki/Mid-Ohio_Sports_Car_Course "Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course") road courses opened in the 1950s and 1960s respectively. Other motorsport venues in the Midwest are [Indianapolis Raceway Park](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Raceway_at_Indianapolis "Lucas Oil Raceway at Indianapolis") (home of the [NHRA U.S. Nationals](/wiki/NHRA_U.S._Nationals "NHRA U.S. Nationals")), [Michigan International Speedway](/wiki/Michigan_International_Speedway "Michigan International Speedway"), [Chicagoland Speedway](/wiki/Chicagoland_Speedway "Chicagoland Speedway"), [Kansas Speedway](/wiki/Kansas_Speedway "Kansas Speedway"), [Gateway International Raceway](/wiki/Gateway_International_Raceway "Gateway International Raceway"), and the [Iowa Speedway](/wiki/Iowa_Speedway "Iowa Speedway"). The [Kentucky Speedway](/wiki/Kentucky_Speedway "Kentucky Speedway") is just outside the officially defined Midwest, but is linked with the region because the track is located in the [Cincinnati metropolitan area](/wiki/Cincinnati_metropolitan_area "Cincinnati metropolitan area").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Notable professional golf tournaments in the Midwest include the [Memorial Tournament](/wiki/Memorial_Tournament "Memorial Tournament"), [BMW Championship](/wiki/BMW_Championship_(PGA_Tour) "BMW Championship (PGA Tour)") and [John Deere Classic](/wiki/John_Deere_Classic "John Deere Classic").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cultural overlap

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Mount_Rushmore_Closeup_2017.jpg/250px-Mount_Rushmore_Closeup_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Rushmore_Closeup_2017.jpg)

[Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") is located in the [Black Hills](/wiki/Black_Hills,_South_Dakota "Black Hills, South Dakota") of [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota").

Differences in the definition of the Midwest mainly split between the Great Plains region on one side, and the Great Lakes region on the other. Although some point to the small towns and agricultural communities in Kansas, Iowa, the Dakotas, and Nebraska of the Great Plains as representative of traditional Midwestern lifestyles and values, others assert that the industrial cities of the Great Lakes—with their histories of 19th century and early 20th century immigration, manufacturing base, and strong Catholic influence—are more representative of the Midwestern experience. In South Dakota, for instance, [West River](/wiki/West_River_(South_Dakota) "West River (South Dakota)") (the region west of the Missouri River) shares cultural elements with the western United States, while [East River](/wiki/East_River_(South_Dakota) "East River (South Dakota)") has more in common with the rest of the Midwest.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

Two other regions, [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") and the Ozark Mountains, overlap geographically with the Midwest—Appalachia in Southern Ohio and the Ozarks in Southern Missouri. The Ohio River has long been a boundary between North and [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") and between the Midwest and the [Upper South](/wiki/Upper_South "Upper South"). All of the lower Midwestern states, especially Missouri, have major Southern components and influences, as they neighbor the Southern region. Historically, Missouri was a [slave](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") state before the American Civil War (1861–1865) due to the [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise "Missouri Compromise").

[Western Pennsylvania](/wiki/Western_Pennsylvania "Western Pennsylvania"), which contains the cities of [Erie](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania") and [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), shares history with the Midwest, and overlaps with Appalachia and the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") as well.[[217]](#cite_note-217)

[Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") is not considered part of the Midwest; it is a northern region of [the South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), although certain northern parts of the state could have possibly been grouped with the Midwest in a geographical context, even though it is geographically in the Southeast overall.[[218]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-218) Kentucky is categorized as Southern by the U.S. Census Bureau due to its industries and especially from a historical and cultural standpoint with the majority of the state having a thoroughly majority Southern accent, demographic, history, and culture in line with her sister states of Virginia and Tennessee and even the areas that have certain Midwestern influences tend to be mixed with the native Southern culture of the area.[[219]](#cite_note-219)[[220]](#cite_note-220)

In addition to intra-American regional overlaps, the [Upper Peninsula of Michigan](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") has historically had strong cultural ties to Canada, partly as a result of early settlement by [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians"). Moreover, the [Yooper accent](/wiki/Yooper_accent "Yooper accent") shares some traits with [Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English"), further demonstrating transnational cultural connections. Similar but less pronounced mutual Canadian-American cultural influence occurs throughout the Great Lakes region.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
