The Midwestern United States is a politically divided region, with the Democratic Party being stronger in the [Great Lakes Region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Region "Great Lakes Region") and the Republican Party being stronger in the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") regions. The Upper Midwestern states of Illinois, Michigan, and Wisconsin reliably voted Democratic in every presidential election from 1992 to 2012. Meanwhile, Minnesota has the longest Democratic voting streak in the nation, having last voted for a Republican presidential candidate in 1972. Recently, Republicans have made serious inroads in Iowa and Ohio, two states that were previously considered swing states.

Missouri has been won by Republicans in every presidential election since 2000, despite its [former bellwether status](/wiki/Missouri_Bellwether "Missouri Bellwether"). Indiana has been won by Republicans in every presidential election since 1940, except for Lyndon Johnson in [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election") and Barack Obama in [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election").[[221]](#cite_note-221) The Great Plains states of North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, and Kansas have voted for the Republican candidate in every presidential election since 1940, except for Democrat [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") in [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). The unicameral [Nebraska Legislature](/wiki/Nebraska_Legislature "Nebraska Legislature") is officially nonpartisan.[[222]](#cite_note-222)

All Midwestern states use primary elections to select delegates for both the Democratic and Republican national conventions, except for Iowa. The [Iowa caucuses](/wiki/Iowa_caucuses "Iowa caucuses") in early January of leap years are the first votes in the [presidential nominating process](/wiki/United_States_presidential_election "United States presidential election") for both major parties, and attract enormous media attention.[[223]](#cite_note-223)

### East North Central

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: East North Central")]

As of 2025, the state government of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") currently has a Democratic Governor [J.B. Pritzker](/wiki/J._B._Pritzker "J. B. Pritzker") and [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") supermajorities in both houses of the [Illinois General Assembly](/wiki/Illinois_General_Assembly "Illinois General Assembly"). Illinois also has 2 Democratic U.S. senators and a 14–3 Democratic majority [U.S. House delegation](/wiki/Illinois%27s_congressional_delegations "Illinois's congressional delegations").

As of 2025, Wisconsin has a Democratic Governor [Tony Evers](/wiki/Tony_Evers "Tony Evers") and a Republican-controlled [Wisconsin Legislature](/wiki/Wisconsin_Legislature "Wisconsin Legislature"). Wisconsin also has 1 Democratic and 1 Republican Senator and a 6-2 Republican majority [U.S. House delegation](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_congressional_delegations "Wisconsin's congressional delegations"). Wisconsin is considered a [purple state](/wiki/Purple_state "Purple state"), as the state was decided by less than 1 percentage point in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2016 United States presidential election in Wisconsin"), [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2020 United States presidential election in Wisconsin"), and [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2024 United States presidential election in Wisconsin"), with the nationwide winner also taking Wisconsin each time.

From [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election_in_Michigan "1992 United States presidential election in Michigan") to [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election_in_Michigan "2012 United States presidential election in Michigan"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") consistently voted for the Democratic presidential candidate, though in four of those six elections, the margin of victory was in the single digits. Beginning in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Michigan "2016 United States presidential election in Michigan"), it has evolved into a true swing state, with [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") winning Michigan in his two successful presidential bids in 2016 and [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Michigan "2024 United States presidential election in Michigan"). The [Michigan Legislature](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") is also closely divided; the Democrats currently hold the minimum 20-18 majority in the [Senate](/wiki/Michigan_Senate "Michigan Senate") and the Republicans hold a narrow 58-52 majority in the [House](/wiki/Michigan_House_of_Representatives "Michigan House of Representatives"). As of 2025, Michigan has 2 Democratic U.S. Senators and a 7-6 bare Republican majority in their [U.S. House of Representatives delegation](/wiki/United_States_congressional_delegations_from_Michigan "United States congressional delegations from Michigan").

Indiana is considered a Republican stronghold, having voting for that party's presidential candidate in every election since 1940, except for [Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") in 1964 and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in 2008. As of 2025, the Republican party controls both U.S. Senate seats, has a 7–2 majority [U.S. House congressional delegation](/wiki/Indiana%27s_congressional_delegations "Indiana's congressional delegations"), and has a state-level trifecta (the governorship and both houses of the [Indiana General Assembly](/wiki/Indiana_General_Assembly "Indiana General Assembly")).

As of 2025, Ohio currently has a Republican Governor [Mike DeWine](/wiki/Mike_DeWine "Mike DeWine") and Republican majorities in the [Ohio General Assembly](/wiki/Ohio_General_Assembly "Ohio General Assembly"). Ohio also has 2 Republican U.S. Senators and a 10-5 Republican majority U.S. House delegation. Ohio has been a battleground state in presidential elections, and no Republican has won the office without winning Ohio. [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") won Ohio by about 8 percentage points in both the [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Ohio "2016 United States presidential election in Ohio") and [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_Ohio "2020 United States presidential election in Ohio") presidential elections, signaling a shift towards the right for the state's federal electorate. The [2022 midterms](/wiki/2022_United_States_elections "2022 United States elections") resulted in strong Republican support at the state level, and moderate Republican support at the federal level, with Republican governor Mike DeWine winning reelection in a [landslide](/wiki/2022_Ohio_gubernatorial_election "2022 Ohio gubernatorial election") and Republican author [JD Vance](/wiki/JD_Vance "JD Vance") winning election [to the U.S. Senate](/wiki/2022_United_States_Senate_election_in_Ohio "2022 United States Senate election in Ohio") by about 6 percentage points. Ohio's rightward shift continued in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Ohio "2024 United States presidential election in Ohio"), with Trump (and Vance as his running mate) once again winning the Buckeye State, this time increasing his margin of victory to more than 11 percentage points, becoming the first presidential candidate to win the state by double digits since [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in [1988](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election_in_Ohio "1988 United States presidential election in Ohio") and having the largest margin of victory for any candidate since the [1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election_in_Ohio "1984 United States presidential election in Ohio") landslide reelection of [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"). Additionally, Republican challenger [Bernie Moreno](/wiki/Bernie_Moreno "Bernie Moreno") defeated three-term incumbent Democrat [Sherrod Brown](/wiki/Sherrod_Brown "Sherrod Brown") in the state's [Senate election](/wiki/2024_United_States_Senate_election_in_Ohio "2024 United States Senate election in Ohio"). With Moreno's election, this meant that with the exception of [Jennifer Brunner](/wiki/Jennifer_Brunner "Jennifer Brunner") on the state [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Ohio "Supreme Court of Ohio"), all statewide elected officials in Ohio are now Republicans.

### West North Central

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: West North Central")]

The Great Plains states of North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, and Kansas have been strongholds for the Republicans for many decades. These four states have gone for the Republican candidate in every presidential election since 1940, except for [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson")'s landslide over [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") in [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). Although North Dakota and South Dakota have often elected Democrats to Congress, after the 2012 election both states' congressional delegations are majority Republican. Nebraska has elected Democrats to the Senate and as governor in recent years, but both of its senators have been Republican since the retirement of [Ben Nelson](/wiki/Ben_Nelson "Ben Nelson") in 2012. Kansas has elected a majority of Democrats as governor since 1956, but has not elected a Democratic senator since 1932. From 1997 to 2010 and again since 2019, Kansas has had at least one Democratic House member (two in 2007 and '08).

Iowa had a Democratic governor from 1999 until [Terry Branstad](/wiki/Terry_Branstad "Terry Branstad") was re-elected in the mid-term elections in 2010, and has had both one Democratic and one Republican senator since the early 1980s until the 2014 election when Republican [Joni Ernst](/wiki/Joni_Ernst "Joni Ernst") defeated Democrat [Bruce Braley](/wiki/Bruce_Braley "Bruce Braley") in a tightly contested race.[[224]](#cite_note-224) Between 1988 and 2012, Iowa also voted for the Democratic presidential candidate in all elections except 2004 (backing [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") by less than 1%), but in 2016 and 2020 [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") won the state by about 9 and 8 percentage points, respectively. Since the 2016 elections, Republicans have held a majority in both houses of the [Iowa General Assembly](/wiki/Iowa_General_Assembly "Iowa General Assembly"). Following the [2022 elections](/wiki/2022_Iowa_elections "2022 Iowa elections"), Iowa is considered a [red state](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states") as Republicans hold all but one statewide office, both U.S. Senate seats, all four U.S. House seats, and Republican governor [Kim Reynolds](/wiki/Kim_Reynolds "Kim Reynolds") was [reelected](/wiki/2022_Iowa_gubernatorial_election "2022 Iowa gubernatorial election") by a margin of nearly 20 points. Trump won Iowa for a third time in 2024, this time by more than 13 percentage points, the largest margin of victory for a Republican in the state since [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in 1972.

Minnesota voters have not voted for a Republican candidate for president since 1972, longer than any other state. Minnesota was the only state (along with [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")) to vote for its native son [Walter Mondale](/wiki/Walter_Mondale "Walter Mondale") over [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") in 1984. However, recent Democratic victories have often been fairly narrow, such as the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Minnesota "2016 United States presidential election in Minnesota"). The Democratic Party controls all statewide offices as of 2025 and hold the minimum 34-33 majority in the [State Senate](/wiki/Minnesota_Senate "Minnesota Senate"), but they do not have a governing trifecta since the [State House](/wiki/Minnesota_House_of_Representatives "Minnesota House of Representatives") is evenly split 67-67. The Minnesota congressional delegation has 2 Democratic Senators but [a 4-4 evenly split U.S. House delegation](/wiki/United_States_congressional_delegations_from_Minnesota "United States congressional delegations from Minnesota").

Missouri was historically considered a bellwether state, having voted for the winner in every presidential election from 1904 to 2004 except for [1956](/wiki/1956_United_States_presidential_election_in_Missouri "1956 United States presidential election in Missouri"), when it backed losing Democrat [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II") by 0.22% and less than 4,000 votes. Democrats generally only hold sway in the large cities at the opposite ends of the state, Kansas City and St. Louis, with the Republicans winning the rest of the state. Since the 2012 elections, Republicans have had a 6–2 majority in the state's [U.S. House delegation](/wiki/Missouri%27s_congressional_delegations "Missouri's congressional delegations"), with African-American Democrats representing the two major cities. Missouri has had a Republican governor since the 2016 elections, as well as both U.S. Senators being Republican since the [2018 United States Senate elections](/wiki/2018_United_States_Senate_elections "2018 United States Senate elections"). As of 2025, Republicans have supermajorities in both houses of the [Missouri General Assembly](/wiki/Missouri_General_Assembly "Missouri General Assembly").

* + Sisson, Richard, Christian Zacher, and Andrew Cayton, eds. *The American Midwest: An Interpretive Encyclopedia* (Indiana University Press, 2006), 1916 pp of articles by scholars on all topics covering the 12 states. [online](https://archive.org/details/americanmidwesti0000unse)
